This makes it **rugged, cheap, and maintenance-free**, which is why ~90% of industrial motors in the world are squirrel cage type. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Quantity | Standard Unit | Symbol | |---|---|---| | Supply Frequency | **50 Hz** (India) | $f$ | | Number of Poles | 2, 4, 6, 8 (typical) | $P$ | | Synchronous Speed | 1500, 1000, 750 rpm | $N_s$ | | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | $g$ | | Brake Drum Radius | measured in metres (not cm!) | $R$ | | Rated Output | kW / HP (1 HP = 746 W) | $P_{out}$ | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principle of Operation (Step-by-Step Logic) > [!NOTE] > A 3-phase SCIM never runs at true synchronous speed. The rotor must always "lag" behind the rotating stator magnetic field for torque to be induced. This lag is called **slip**. 1. **Stator Winding Energised:** A balanced 3-phase supply is given to the stator. A **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** is produced at synchronous speed $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ rpm. 2. **Rotor Flux Cutting:** The RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing an EMF (Faraday's Law). 3. **Rotor Current & Torque:** Since the rotor bars are short-circuited, current flows. By **Lenz's Law**, the rotor conductors experience a force ($F = BIL$), producing torque in the direction of RMF rotation. 4. **Rotor Accelerates:** The rotor accelerates in the direction of RMF. 5. **Slip Mechanism:** As rotor speed ($N_r$) approaches $N_s$, relative speed ($N_s - N_r$) reduces, induced EMF drops, rotor current drops, and torque drops. Equilibrium is reached at loaded speed. 6. **Load Application:** When mechanical load is applied via the brake, the rotor slows down slightly → slip increases → more induced EMF → more rotor current → more torque → equilibrium restored at slightly lower speed. ## 2.2 Equivalent Circuit Insights The induction motor behaves electrically like a **transformer with a rotating, short-circuited secondary**. The mechanical load on the shaft is electrically equivalent to a variable resistance $\frac{R_2'}{s}$ in the rotor branch. * At **no-load**: $s \approx 0$, so $\frac{R_2'}{s} \to \infty$ → rotor current is very small. * At **standstill (blocked-rotor)**: $s = 1$, so $\frac{R_2'}{s} = R_2'$ → rotor current is very large (like a short-circuited transformer). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they cover 100% of KTU exam numericals for this experiment.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | Always greater than $N_r$ | | 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per unit (p.u.) or % | At no-load, $s < 0.01$ | | 3 | Slip (in rpm) | $\Delta N = N_s - N_r$ | rpm | Used in torque formulas | | 4 | Input Power (2-Wattmeter) | $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ | W | Positive for normal operation | | 5 | Input Power (3-Wattmeter) | $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 + W_3$ | W | More accurate | | 6 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L$ | VA | $V_L$ = Line Voltage | | 7 | Power Factor | $\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L}$ | dimensionless | Should be 0.7 - 0.9 loaded | | 8 | Output Torque (Rope Brake) | $T_{out} = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g$ | N·m | $W$ = dead weight, $S$ = spring balance, $R$ = radius | | 9 | Output Power (mechanical) | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_r T_{out}}{60}$ | W | Often expressed in kW | | 10 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Maximum at ~75% of full load | | 11 | Slip Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | Rotor EMF frequency | | 12 | Synchronous Angular Velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60}$ | rad/s | For torque calcs | | 13 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\omega_r} = \dfrac{60 \cdot P_{out}}{2 \pi N_r}$ | N·m | Also = Air-gap torque minus losses | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Industrial Commissioning:** Used during factory acceptance tests (FAT) to verify manufacturer datasheet performance. * **Motor Rewinding Shops:** Confirms that the rewound motor has not lost its original efficiency. * **Energy Auditing (BEE Star Rating):** Efficiency curves are mandatory for IS 12615 / IE3 / IE4 class compliance. * **Pump, Fan, Compressor Selection:** Torque-speed curves ensure the motor can handle starting and running duty of the driven load. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Rope-Brake Output Torque Consider a brake drum mounted on the motor shaft. A rope is wrapped around the drum with one end attached to a **dead weight $W$ (kgf)** and the other end to a **spring balance $S$ (kgf)**. **Step 1 — Identify the forces on the drum:** The net tangential force on the drum surface (in kgf) is the difference between the two rope tensions: $$F_{net} = (W - S)$$ **Step 2 — Convert kgf to Newtons:** Since $1 \text{ kgf} = g \text{ Newtons} = 9.81 \text{ N}$: $$F_{net} = (W - S) \cdot g \;\; \text{Newtons}$$ **Step 3 — Compute torque using moment-arm = drum radius $R$:** $$T_{out} = F_{net} \times R = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g$$ $$\boxed{T_{out} = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g \;\; \text{N·m}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget the factor $g = 9.81$. They write $T = (W-S) \cdot R$, which is **wrong dimensionally** (gives kgf·m, not N·m). Always convert kgf → N. ## 3.2 Derivation: Output Mechanical Power Power = Force × Velocity. For rotational motion: $$P = T \cdot \omega$$ **Step 1 — Angular velocity of rotor:** $$\omega_r = \frac{2 \pi N_r}{60} \;\; \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into power equation:** $$P_{out} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_r = T_{out} \cdot \frac{2 \pi N_r}{60}$$ $$\boxed{P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N_r T_{out}}{60} \;\; \text{Watts}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Per-Unit Slip $$s_{pu} = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ To express as a percentage, multiply by 100: $$s_{\%} = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \times 100$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU Style) **Problem:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor draws 8 A at 400 V with a total input of 4.2 kW at full load. Rope brake readings: $W = 5$ kgf, $S = 1$ kgf, drum radius $R = 7.5$ cm = 0.075 m. Tachometer reads 1410 rpm. Calculate: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Slip, (iii) Output torque, (iv) Output power, (v) Efficiency, (vi) Power factor. **Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:** **(i) Synchronous Speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **(ii) Slip:** $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1410}{1500} = \frac{90}{1500} = 0.06 \text{ p.u.} = 6\%$$ **(iii) Output Torque:** $$T_{out} = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g = (5 - 1) \times 0.075 \times 9.81$$ $$T_{out} = 4 \times 0.075 \times 9.81 = 2.943 \text{ N·m}$$ **(iv) Output Power:** $$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N_r T_{out}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1410 \times 2.943}{60}$$ $$P_{out} = \frac{26074.4}{60} = 434.57 \text{ W} = 0.4346 \text{ kW}$$ **(v) Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{434.57}{4200} \times 100 = 10.35\%$$ **(vi) Power Factor:** $$\cos \phi = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L} = \frac{4200}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 8}$$ $$\cos \phi = \frac{4200}{5542.56} = 0.758 \text{ lagging}$$ ## 3.5 KTU Observation Table Template | S.No. | $V_L$ (V) | $I_L$ (A) | $W_1$ (W) | $W_2$ (W) | $W$ (kgf) | $S$ (kgf) | $N_r$ (rpm) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 400 | 3.5 | 200 | 100 | 0 | 0 | 1490 | | 2 | 400 | 4.5 | 350 | 180 | 1.5 | 0.3 | 1475 | | 3 | 400 | 6.0 | 700 | 400 | 3.0 | 0.7 | 1450 | | 4 | 400 | 8.0 | 1100 | 700 | 5.0 | 1.2 | 1410 | | 5 | 400 | 11.5 | 1600 | 1100 | 7.5 | 2.0 | 1360 | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Circuit Diagram (2-Wattmeter Method + Rope Brake) ```mermaid flowchart LR RYB[3-Phase Supply R Y B] --> MCB[MCB / TP Switch] MCB --> V1[Voltmeter V across R-Y] MCB --> A1[Ammeter A in R-line] A1 --> W1C[W1 Current Coil in R] W1C --> M1[Motor Terminal U] MCB --> W2C[W2 Current Coil in Y] W2C --> M2[Motor Terminal V] MCB --> M3[Motor Terminal W] W1P[W1 Pressure Coil] -.-> R1[Junction R-Y] W2P[W2 Pressure Coil] -.-> Y1[Junction Y-B] M1 --> SHAFT((Motor Shaft)) SHAFT --> DRUM[Brake Drum R radius] DRUM --> ROPE[Rope 1 Dead Weight W] DRUM --> ROPE2[Rope 2 Spring Balance S] TACH[Tachometer Sensor] -.-> SHAFT ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Load Test Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POWER["ELECTRICAL INPUT STAGE"] SP[3-Phase AC Mains 400V 50Hz] --> SW[DOL Starter / TP MCB] SW --> METER["METERING BANK
V, A, W1, W2"] end METER --> MOTOR["3-PHASE SCIM
Stator Rotor Cage"] MOTOR --> COUPLE[Shaft Coupling] subgraph MECH["MECHANICAL LOAD STAGE"] COUPLE --> BRAKE["ROPE BRAKE
W - S x R x g"] BRAKE --> DUMP[Heat Dissipation to Air] end MOTOR -. Tacho .-> TACH[RPM Measurement] METER --> PC[Data Logging / Calculation] BRAKE --> PC TACH --> PC PC --> GRAPH["PERFORMANCE CURVES
eta vs P, s vs P, p.f. vs P"] ``` ## 4.3 Expected Performance Curve Shapes (Mermaid X-Y Style) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CURVES["TYPICAL GRAPHS"] G1["Slip vs P_out: Roughly Linear, Rising"] G2["Efficiency vs P_out: Inverted Parabola, Peak at 75% FL"] G3["Power Factor vs P_out: Rising, approaching 0.85-0.9 at FL"] G4["Speed vs P_out: Slight Linear Drop, ~2-5% drop"] G5["Torque vs P_out: Linear, Proportional"] G6["Line Current vs P_out: Roughly Linear, Rising"] end ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define slip in an induction motor. Why is it impossible for the rotor to run at synchronous speed?** **Model Answer:** Slip $s$ is defined as the **relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor, expressed as a fraction (or percentage) of synchronous speed**. $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ > [!NOTE] > If $N_r = N_s$, then there is **no relative motion** between the rotor conductors and the stator flux. Hence, **no EMF is induced**, **no rotor current flows**, and **no torque is produced**. Therefore, the rotor cannot accelerate to $N_s$ — it must always lag behind by a small amount (typically 2–8%) to maintain torque. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any three methods of applying mechanical load during a load test on an induction motor.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Rope Brake (Friction Brake)** — most common in KTU labs; uses rope, drum, dead weight, spring balance. 2. **Eddy Current Dynamometer** — electromagnetic, smooth control, used in industry. 3. **Powder Brake / Hysteresis Brake** — for small fractional kW motors. 4. **DC Generator Loading** — motor drives a DC shunt generator whose loading is varied by a rheostat (electrical loading instead of mechanical friction). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the procedure to conduct a load test on a **3-phase squirrel cage induction motor** using the **two-wattmeter method**. List the instruments used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The following readings were obtained during the load test of a 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase induction motor: $V_L = 400$ V, $I_L = 10$ A, $W_1 = 2500$ W, $W_2 = 1500$ W, $W = 6$ kgf, $S = 1.5$ kgf, $R = 0.1$ m, $N_r = 1420$ rpm. Calculate (i) Synchronous speed (ii) Slip (iii) Input power (iv) Output torque (v) Output power (vi) Efficiency (vii) Power factor. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Procedure & Circuit:** 1. **Connections:** Connect the 3-phase supply through a **TP MCCB / DOL starter** to the stator terminals (U, V, W) of the SCIM. 2. **Voltmeter:** Connect across any two lines (e.g., R-Y). 3. **Ammeter:** Connect in series with one line (R). 4. **Two Wattmeters:** Connect in the standard 2-wattmeter configuration — current coils in lines R and Y, pressure coils between (R-Y) and (Y-B). 5. **Brake Setup:** Mount the rope brake drum on the shaft. Wrap a cotton rope with one end tied to a **dead weight $W$** and the other to a **spring balance $S$**. Keep a water container or cooling arrangement to dissipate heat. 6. **Tachometer:** Either digital photo-tachometer or analog tachogenerator on the shaft end. **Procedure Steps:** * Switch on supply; check direction of rotation. * Take **no-load readings** first ($W \approx 0$, $S \approx 0$). * Gradually increase load by tightening the rope or adding weights in steps. * For each step, record: $V_L$, $I_L$, $W_1$, $W_2$, $W$, $S$, $N_r$. * Continue until **rated current** is reached. * Take readings on unloading for verification. * Switch off supply. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Synchronous Speed:** $$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$ **(ii) Slip:** $$s = \frac{1500 - 1420}{1500} = \frac{80}{1500} = 0.0533 = 5.33\%$$ **(iii) Input Power:** $$P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 = 2500 + 1500 = 4000 \text{ W} = 4 \text{ kW}$$ **(iv) Output Torque:** $$T_{out} = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g = (6 - 1.5) \times 0.1 \times 9.81$$ $$T_{out} = 4.5 \times 0.1 \times 9.81 = 4.4145 \text{ N·m}$$ **(v) Output Power:** $$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N_r T_{out}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1420 \times 4.4145}{60}$$ $$P_{out} = \frac{39386.5}{60} = 656.44 \text{ W} \approx 0.656 \text{ kW}$$ **(vi) Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{656.44}{4000} \times 100 = 16.41\%$$ **(vii) Power Factor:** $$\cos \phi = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L} = \frac{4000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10} = \frac{4000}{6928.2} = 0.5774 \text{ lagging}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error in the answer script is using **rated $P_{out}$ from the nameplate** instead of computing it from the **rope-brake formula**. Always compute output power from the brake readings — that is the *measured* output. Nameplate rating is only for reference. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **construction and working principle of a 3-phase squirrel cage induction motor** with a neat diagram. Why is it called a "squirrel cage"? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 6-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase SCIM takes a no-load input of 600 W at 3 A and a full-load input of 5500 W at 18 A from a 400 V supply. Rope brake data at full load: $W = 10$ kgf, $S = 2.5$ kgf, $R = 0.08$ m, $N_r = 940$ rpm. Calculate: (i) No-load power factor (ii) Full-load slip (iii) Full-load output torque (iv) Full-load efficiency (v) Full-load power factor. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction & Working:** **Stator:** * Laminated silicon-steel core with semi-enclosed/closed slots. * Three-phase distributed winding embedded in slots. * Connected in star or delta. **Rotor:** * Laminated cylindrical core mounted on shaft. * **Rotor bars** (aluminium or copper) placed in slots, parallel to shaft axis. * All bars are **permanently short-circuited at both ends by conducting end-rings**. > [!NOTE] > **Why "squirrel cage"?** The rotor resembles the running wheel inside a hamster cage — parallel bars connected by end rings. There are **no slip rings**, **no brushes**, and **no external electrical connections** to the rotor. Hence it is called a **squirrel cage rotor** and is extremely rugged. **Working:** A balanced 3-phase stator supply produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at $N_s = 120f/P$. The RMF cuts rotor bars, induces EMF, current flows through shorted bars, and force $F = BIL$ produces torque in the direction of RMF rotation. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) No-load Power Factor:** $$\cos \phi_{NL} = \frac{P_{NL}}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_{NL}} = \frac{600}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 3} = \frac{600}{2078.46} = 0.2887 \text{ lagging}$$ **(ii) Full-load Slip:** $$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$ $$s_{FL} = \frac{1000 - 940}{1000} = \frac{60}{1000} = 0.06 = 6\%$$ **(iii) Full-load Output Torque:** $$T_{out} = (10 - 2.5) \times 0.08 \times 9.81 = 7.5 \times 0.08 \times 9.81 = 5.886 \text{ N·m}$$ **(iv) Full-load Efficiency:** $$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi \times 940 \times 5.886}{60} = \frac{34758}{60} = 579.3 \text{ W}$$ $$\eta_{FL} = \frac{579.3}{5500} \times 100 = 10.53\%$$ **(v) Full-load Power Factor:** $$\cos \phi_{FL} = \frac{5500}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 18} = \frac{5500}{12470.77} = 0.4410 \text{ lagging}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't mix star/delta formulas** — always use **line values** $V_L$, $I_L$ for total 3-phase calculations. > 2. **Don't forget the $g = 9.81$ factor** in rope brake torque — leads to 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Sign of $W_2$**: In 2-wattmeter method, if $W_2$ reads *negative* (low power factor operation), reverse the pressure coil, record as negative, and use $P_{in} = W_1 - W_2$. If $W_1$ is also negative, use $P_{in} = -(W_1 + W_2)$. > 4. **Diameter vs Radius**: Always measure **radius in metres** — diameter-based errors are the #1 reason students lose 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Slip must always exist** — without slip, no torque. - ✅ **$N_s = 120f/P$** — synchronous speed depends only on frequency and poles, *not* on load. - ✅ **Efficiency peaks at ~75% of full load** for standard motors. - ✅ **No-load current is 30–40% of full-load current**; no-load p.f. is very poor (0.1–0.3) due to magnetising reactive current. - ✅ **Rotor bars of SCIM are skewed** — prevents cogging (magnetic locking) and reduces harmonics. - ✅ **Rope brake heat dissipation** — water drips on drum are common in labs; otherwise the rope will burn out. - ✅ **Two-wattmeter method is valid** only if the load is balanced and connected in star or delta without neutral wire. - ✅ **Always tabulate and graph** — KTU asks for performance curves in viva and record. - ✅ **Slip increases with load** — but only slightly (typically from 0.5% to 5%). - ✅ **Power factor improves with load** — from 0.2 no-load to 0.85+ at full load. - ✅ **Units check** — final $P_{out}$ in **Watts**, $\eta$ in **%**, slip in **p.u. or %**, torque in **N·m**. ---

topics.title: Load test on three phase slip ring induction motor
Starting of three phase induction motor using star-delta/autotransformer methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUL607 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND MECHATRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Starting of three phase induction motor using star-delta/autotransformer methods # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three-Phase Induction Motor Starting:** > The process of bringing a squirrel-cage / slip-ring induction motor from rest to a safe operating speed by limiting the **inrush (starting) current** drawn from the supply and providing adequate **starting torque** to overcome load friction and inertia, without violating thermal and mechanical stress limits of the motor and the connected network. The two reduced-voltage starting methods covered under this experiment are: | Sl. No. | Starting Method | Standard Reference | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Star-Delta (Y–Δ) Starter** | Used only for motors designed for **delta running** with 6 accessible terminals (U1, V1, W1, U2, V2, W2) | | 2 | **Autotransformer Starter** | Universal method; reduced voltage obtained by **tapped autotransformer** (typical taps: 50 %, 65 %, 80 %) | ## 1.2 Why Starters Are Needed — The DOL Problem A 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor, when switched Direct-On-Line (DOL), draws a starting current of **5 to 7 times** its full-load current, while the starting torque is only **1.0 to 2.0 times** the full-load torque. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Industry Rule of Thumb:** > - **DOL Starting Current:** $I_{sc} \approx 5$ to $7 \times I_{FL}$ > - **DOL Starting Torque:** $T_{st} \approx 1.5$ to $2.0 \times T_{FL}$ The consequences of DOL starting large motors are: - **Voltage dip** in the supply network affecting other loads. - **Heating** of stator windings during prolonged starting. - **Mechanical shock** to couplings, belts and gearboxes. - **Tripping** of upstream protective devices (MCB / MCCB / relays). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!TIP] > **Analogy: Pushing a Heavy Cart on a Slope** > > Imagine pushing a **loaded cart up a hill**. If you push it with **maximum force right from rest**, the cart jerks violently and you may lose balance or injure yourself. Instead, you first **push gently** (low torque, low "current") so the cart starts rolling, and then **gradually increase the push** as it gains momentum. > > A **reduced-voltage starter** does exactly this for the induction motor: > - **Low voltage** at start = gentle push = less inrush current. > - **Voltage is raised** (or motor is switched to full voltage) once the rotor has gained speed. > > - **Star-Delta** = like shifting gears on a bicycle (low gear to start, high gear to cruise). > - **Autotransformer** = like using a **variable cushion** (you can choose 50 %, 65 % or 80 % cushion based on load). ## 1.4 Application Map — Where These Starters Are Used | Starter Type | Typical Motor Rating | Industry Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Star-Delta | 5 HP – 50 HP (light/medium load) | Centrifugal pumps, fans, compressors, conveyors | | Autotransformer | 20 HP – 500 HP (heavy load) | Rolling mills, crushers, large blowers, elevators | --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Star-Delta (Y–Δ) Starting Method ### Operating Principle The stator windings of an induction motor designed for **delta running** are initially connected in **star** during starting. After the rotor accelerates to ~70–80 % of synchronous speed, a timer-operated switching changes the connection to **delta** for normal running. ### Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — At Rest (Star Mode):** - The three stator winding "tails" (U2, V2, W2) are shorted together to form the **star point**. - The three "heads" (U1, V1, W1) are connected to the 3-phase lines R, Y, B. - Each phase winding receives only $\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ instead of the full line voltage. **Step 2 — Acceleration in Star:** - Reduced phase voltage → reduced starting current. - Motor accelerates smoothly up to ~70–80 % of $N_s$. **Step 3 — Transition (Open Transition):** - A timer (typically 3–10 s) opens the **star contactor** and closes the **delta contactor**. - Brief open-circuit moment exists — modern closed-transition starters avoid this. **Step 4 — Run Mode (Delta Mode):** - Motor windings now connected in delta; full line voltage across each phase. - Motor continues to accelerate to full-load speed. ### Required Hardware | Component | Symbol | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Main Contactor | K1 | Connects supply to motor terminals | | Star Contactor | K2 | Shorts U2-V2-W2 to form star point | | Delta Contactor | K3 | Connects windings in delta | | Timer Relay | T | Controls transition time | | Overload Relay | OLR | Protects against sustained overcurrent | --- ## 2.2 The Autotransformer Starting Method ### Operating Principle A **three-phase autotransformer** with multiple taps (typically **50 %, 65 %, 80 %**) is inserted between the supply and the motor terminals. At start, the motor receives a **reduced voltage** equal to the tap voltage. After acceleration, the autotransformer is **bypassed** and the motor is connected directly to the supply. ### Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — At Rest (Tap Connected):** - Main contactor K1 closes; tap contactor K2 closes. - Motor receives voltage $V_{motor} = x \cdot V_L$ where $x$ = tap ratio (0.5 / 0.65 / 0.8). **Step 2 — Acceleration in Tapped Mode:** - Reduced voltage → reduced starting current in motor. - Motor accelerates; current drops as speed increases. **Step 3 — Bypass Transition:** - Timer activates K3 (bypass contactor) and de-energises K2. - Motor now sees full line voltage $V_L$. **Step 4 — Run Mode (Autotransformer Isolated):** - K1 and K3 remain closed; K2 open. - Motor runs at full voltage on direct supply. ### Key Engineering Insight > [!IMPORTANT] > Because power is conserved in an ideal autotransformer (ignoring magnetising losses), the current drawn from the **supply** is the **square** of the tap ratio times the DOL current — much greater reduction than star-delta at equivalent voltage. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Notation used:** $V_L$ = Line Voltage, $I_L$ = Line Current, $V_{ph}$ = Phase Voltage, $I_{ph}$ = Phase Current, $Z_{ph}$ = Standstill phase impedance, $x$ = Tap ratio $(0 < x < 1)$. ### Table 2.1 — Complete Formula Reference | # | Quantity | DOL (Δ direct) | Star Mode (Y) | Autotransformer Mode (tap $x$) | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Phase Voltage on winding | $V_{ph} = V_L$ | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $V_{ph} = x \cdot V_L$ | V | | 2 | Phase (motor) starting current | $I_{ph,DOL} = \dfrac{V_L}{Z_{ph}}$ | $I_{ph,Y} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \, Z_{ph}}$ | $I_{ph,auto} = \dfrac{x \, V_L}{Z_{ph}}$ | A | | 3 | Line current from supply | $I_{L,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \, I_{ph,DOL}$ | $I_{L,Y} = I_{ph,Y}$ | $I_{L,auto} = x^2 \, I_{L,DOL}$ | A | | 4 | Starting current ratio $\dfrac{I_{L,reduced}}{I_{L,DOL}}$ | $1$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ | $x^2$ | — | | 5 | Starting torque $T \propto \dfrac{V_{ph}^2}{Z_{ph}^2}$ | $T_{DOL} = k \, \dfrac{V_L^2}{Z_{ph}^2}$ | $T_Y = k \, \dfrac{V_L^2}{3 \, Z_{ph}^2}$ | $T_{auto} = k \, \dfrac{x^2 \, V_L^2}{Z_{ph}^2}$ | Nm | | 6 | Starting torque ratio $\dfrac{T_{reduced}}{T_{DOL}}$ | $1$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ | $x^2$ | — | | 7 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Same | Same | RPM | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Observation (must be memorised for KTU exam):** > - In **Star-Delta**, both current and torque reduce by factor $\dfrac{1}{3} \approx 33.3\%$. > - In **Autotransformer**, both current and torque reduce by factor $x^2$ (tap-squared). > - For **65 % tap**: $x^2 = 0.4225$ → current reduction to 42.25 %. > - **Star-Delta is equivalent to autotransformer with $x = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577$ (57.7 %).** ### Table 2.2 — Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Star-Delta Starter | Autotransformer Starter | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Initial voltage applied | $\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ (fixed) | $x \cdot V_L$ (variable: 50/65/80 %) | | Starting current reduction | Fixed at $\dfrac{1}{3}$ | Adjustable ($x^2$: 0.25 / 0.4225 / 0.64) | | Starting torque reduction | Fixed at $\dfrac{1}{3}$ | Adjustable ($x^2$) | | Tap flexibility | None | 3 taps available | | Cost | Low | Higher (transformer cost) | | Closed transition possible | No (standard); Yes with extra resistor | Yes (more easily) | | Motor terminal requirement | 6 terminals (U1-U2, V1-V2, W1-W2) | 3 terminals only | | Typical HP range | 5 – 50 HP | 20 – 500 HP | | Number of starts/hour | Moderate | Higher (less heating) | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power utilities** mandate reduced-voltage starting for motors above **5 HP** connected to weak grids to avoid voltage sag. - **Star-Delta** is the **most economical** industrial starter and dominates the **pump and fan industry**. - **Autotransformer starters** are chosen for **high-inertia loads** (e.g., rolling mills) where smooth, adjustable starting torque is essential. - Both methods form the **backbone of industrial motor control panels (MCCs)** and are core components of the **B.Tech Electrical & Mechatronics syllabus** under NEP 2020 outcome-based modules. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Star-Delta Starting Current Ratio ### Given Data - 3-phase induction motor with **delta-connected run windings** and accessible terminals. - Standstill phase impedance: $Z_{ph} = R_2' + jX_2'$ referred to stator per phase. - Line voltage: $V_L$ (RMS, line-to-line). ### Assumption - The motor is idealised as a passive impedance at standstill ($s = 1$). - Magnetising branch current is neglected at the moment of starting. ### Derivation **Step 1: Current in Direct-On-Line (Delta) Mode** When the motor runs in delta, each phase winding sees the **full line voltage**: $$ V_{ph,\Delta} = V_L $$ The per-phase starting current in delta is: $$ I_{ph,\Delta} = \frac{V_L}{Z_{ph}} $$ The line current drawn from the supply in delta is related to phase current by: $$ I_{L,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \, I_{ph,\Delta} $$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{L,DOL} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_L}{Z_{ph}} \\[6pt] \therefore I_{L,DOL} &= \frac{\sqrt{3} \, V_L}{Z_{ph}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Current in Star Mode (During Starting)** When the motor windings are reconnected in star, each phase winding sees a **reduced phase voltage**: $$ V_{ph,Y} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} $$ The per-phase starting current in star is: $$ I_{ph,Y} = \frac{V_{ph,Y}}{Z_{ph}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \, Z_{ph}} $$ In star connection, the **line current equals the phase current**: $$ I_{L,Y} = I_{ph,Y} $$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{L,Y} &= \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \, Z_{ph}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Forming the Ratio** Divide the star starting line current by the DOL starting line current: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{I_{L,Y}}{I_{L,DOL}} &= \frac{\dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \, Z_{ph}}}{\dfrac{\sqrt{3} \, V_L}{Z_{ph}}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \, Z_{ph}} \cdot \frac{Z_{ph}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{3} \end{aligned} $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{ I_{L,Y} = \frac{1}{3} \, I_{L,DOL} } $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning:** In star mode, the starting current drawn from the supply is only **33.33 %** of the DOL inrush current — a **two-thirds reduction**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Star-Delta Starting Torque Ratio ### Starting Torque Equation The electromagnetic torque developed by an induction motor at standstill ($s = 1$) is given by: $$ T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_2'/s}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2} $$ At standstill ($s = 1$), the torque is **proportional to the square of the phase voltage** (assuming $Z_{ph}$ unchanged): $$ T_{st} \propto \frac{V_{ph}^2}{Z_{ph}^2} $$ ### Derivation **Step 1: DOL Starting Torque (Delta)** In delta, $V_{ph,\Delta} = V_L$, therefore: $$ T_{DOL} = k \cdot \frac{V_L^2}{Z_{ph}^2} $$ **Step 2: Star Starting Torque** In star, $V_{ph,Y} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$, therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{Y} &= k \cdot \frac{\left( \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \right)^2}{Z_{ph}^2} \\[6pt] &= k \cdot \frac{V_L^2}{3 \, Z_{ph}^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Forming the Ratio** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{T_{Y}}{T_{DOL}} &= \frac{k \cdot \dfrac{V_L^2}{3 \, Z_{ph}^2}}{k \cdot \dfrac{V_L^2}{Z_{ph}^2}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{3} \end{aligned} $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{ T_{Y} = \frac{1}{3} \, T_{DOL} } $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off in Star-Delta:** Both **starting current AND starting torque** reduce by the **same factor** $\dfrac{1}{3}$. This is acceptable only for loads with low starting torque demand (pumps, fans). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Autotransformer Starting Current Ratio ### Given Data - Autotransformer tap ratio $x$ where $0 < x < 1$ (typical: 0.5, 0.65, 0.8). - $x$ is defined as: $x = \dfrac{V_{motor}}{V_{supply}}$. - Turns ratio $K = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{1}{x}$ (step-down). ### Derivation **Step 1: Voltage Applied to Motor** During starting, the motor receives the reduced tap voltage: $$ V_{motor} = x \cdot V_L $$ **Step 2: Current Drawn by the Motor Winding** The phase voltage on each motor winding is $V_{motor}$, so the current through the motor winding is: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{motor} &= \frac{V_{motor}}{Z_{ph}} = \frac{x \, V_L}{Z_{ph}} \\[6pt] &= x \cdot I_{ph,DOL} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Current Drawn from the Supply (Primary of Autotransformer)** For an ideal autotransformer, input apparent power = output apparent power: $$ \begin{aligned} V_L \cdot I_{line} &= V_{motor} \cdot I_{motor} \\[6pt] I_{line} &= \frac{V_{motor} \cdot I_{motor}}{V_L} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(x \, V_L) \cdot (x \, I_{ph,DOL})}{V_L} \\[6pt] &= x^2 \cdot I_{ph,DOL} \end{aligned} $$ Since $I_{L,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \, I_{ph,DOL}$ in delta running, we express line current more conveniently: $$ I_{line,auto} = \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{3}} \, I_{L,DOL} $$ But more commonly expressed relative to DOL line current using ratio: $$ \boxed{ I_{line,auto} = x^2 \, I_{L,DOL} \quad \text{(approximately, for $I_{ph,DOL}$ basis)} } $$ > [!NOTE] > **For exam purposes, the standard formula is:** > $$\dfrac{I_{L,auto}}{I_{L,DOL}} = x^2$$ **Numerical example for 65 % tap** ($x = 0.65$): $$ x^2 = (0.65)^2 = 0.4225 $$ So starting current reduces to **42.25 %** of DOL. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Autotransformer Starting Torque Ratio The starting torque is proportional to the **square of the phase voltage** on the motor windings: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{T_{auto}}{T_{DOL}} &= \left(\frac{V_{motor}}{V_L}\right)^2 \\[6pt] &= x^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{ T_{auto} = x^2 \, T_{DOL} } $$ > [!TIP] > **Comparison of Torque Reduction:** > | Tap $x$ | $x^2$ | Star-Delta Equivalent? | > | :---: | :---: | :--- | > | 0.50 | 0.25 | No (star-delta gives 0.333) | > | 0.65 | 0.4225 | No | > | 0.80 | 0.64 | No | > | 0.577 ($\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$) | 0.333 | **Yes — exactly equivalent to star-delta** | --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Problem ### Problem Statement A 3-phase, **5 HP**, **400 V**, **50 Hz**, 4-pole squirrel-cage induction motor draws a **DOL starting current of 60 A** and develops a **DOL starting torque of 30 Nm**. Estimate the starting current and starting torque when the motor is started using: **(a)** Star-Delta starter **(b)** Autotransformer starter with **60 % tap** ### Solution Table | Quantity | DOL | Star-Delta | Autotransformer (x = 0.6) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Voltage applied to winding | $V_L = 400$ V | $V_L / \sqrt{3} = 231$ V | $0.6 \times 400 = 240$ V | | Voltage ratio factor | 1 | $1 / \sqrt{3}$ | $0.6$ | | Current ratio factor | 1 | $(1/\sqrt{3})^2 = 1/3$ | $(0.6)^2 = 0.36$ | | **Starting current (A)** | $60$ | $60 / 3 = \mathbf{20}$ | $60 \times 0.36 = \mathbf{21.6}$ | | **Starting torque (Nm)** | $30$ | $30 / 3 = \mathbf{10}$ | $30 \times 0.36 = \mathbf{10.8}$ | ### Interpretation - Star-Delta and 60 % tap autotransformer produce **nearly equal** starting performance. - At slightly higher taps (65 %, 80 %), autotransformer provides **better torque** at the cost of slightly higher current. --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Star-Delta Starter Power Circuit (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD SUP[3-Phase Supply R Y B 400V] --> MCB[MCCB or MCB Protection] MCB --> K1[Main Contactor K1] K1 --> U1[Motor Terminal U1] K1 --> V1[Motor Terminal V1] K1 --> W1[Motor Terminal W1] K1 -.-> K3[Delta Contactor K3 Closed during RUN] K3 --> U2[Motor Terminal U2] K3 --> V2[Motor Terminal V2] K3 --> W2[Motor Terminal W2] K2[Star Contactor K2 Closed during START] --> U2 K2 --> V2 K2 --> W2 U2 -.-> SP[Shorted Star Point during START] V2 -.-> SP W2 -.-> SP DELTA[Delta Link U1-W2 V1-U2 W1-V2 during RUN] U1 --- DELTA V1 --- DELTA W1 --- DELTA TR[Timer Relay] -.controls.-> K1 TR -.controls.-> K2 TR -.controls.-> K3 OLR[Overload Relay OLR] -.monitors.-> K1 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Autotransformer Starter Power Circuit ```mermaid graph TD SUP[3-Phase Supply R Y B] --> ISO[Isolator or MCCB] ISO --> K1[Main Contactor K1] K1 --> AT[3-Phase Autotransformer] AT --> TAP[Tap Selector 50 percent or 65 percent or 80 percent] TAP --> K2[Tap Contactor K2 Closed during START] K2 --> MOTOR[Motor Terminals U V W] K3[Bypass Contactor K3 Closed during RUN] --> MOTOR AT --> NEUT[Common Star Point Neutral] K1 -.energises.-> AT K1 -.connects.-> NEUT TR[Timer Relay] -.controls.-> K1 TR -.controls.-> K2 TR -.controls.-> K3 OLR[Overload Relay] -.protects.-> MOTOR ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Switching Sequence (Star-Delta Operation) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant OP as Operator participant PB as Start Push Button participant TR as Timer Relay participant K1 as Main Contactor participant K2 as Star Contactor participant K3 as Delta Contactor participant M as 3 Phase Motor OP->>PB: Press START PB->>TR: Energise timer coil TR->>K1: Close K1 (supply to motor) K1->>M: Energise stator heads U1 V1 W1 TR->>K2: Close K2 (star mode) K2->>M: Short U2 V2 W2 to star point Note over M: Voltage per phase equals 231V
Motor accelerates in star mode TR->>TR: Wait preset time 3 to 10 seconds TR->>K2: Open K2 (star disconnected) Note over M: Brief open transition moment TR->>K3: Close K3 (delta mode) K3->>M: Reconnect windings in delta U1 to W2 V1 to U2 W1 to V2 Note over M: Voltage per phase equals 400V
Motor runs at full voltage OP->>PB: Press STOP PB->>TR: De-energise timer TR->>K1: Open K1 (supply removed) TR->>K3: Open K3 (motor coasts to stop) ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Lab Setup Block Diagram (Test Bench) ```mermaid graph LR MAINS[3 Phase Mains 415V 50Hz] --> CB[Circuit Breaker] CB --> V1[Voltmeter Line] CB --> A1[Ammeter Line] A1 --> STARTER[Starter Panel Star Delta or AutoTransformer] STARTER --> T1[Tachometer Sensor] STARTER --> M[Induction Motor Coupled to Loading Arrangement] T1 --> DISP[Digital RPM Display] M --> LOAD[Dynamometer or Mechanical Load] LOAD --> TORQ[Torque Sensor] TORQ --> DISPLAY[Digital Torque Readout] V1 --> SCOPE[Oscilloscope or Data Logger] A1 --> SCOPE SCOPE --> GRAPH[Plot Speed vs Time
Plot Current vs Time
Plot Torque vs Slip] ``` ## 4.5 Block Architecture — Experiment Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[Step 1 Identify Motor Name Plate Details] --> B[Step 2 Verify 6 Terminal Connections] B --> C[Step 3 Connect Starter Panel in Star Mode] C --> D[Step 4 Switch ON Supply and Measure Starting Current in Star] D --> E[Step 5 Measure Acceleration Time and Final Speed] E --> F[Step 6 Switch to Delta and Measure Run Current and Speed] F --> G[Step 7 Repeat for Autotransformer Taps 50 65 80 percent] G --> H[Step 8 Tabulate and Compare Results] H --> I[Step 9 Plot Speed vs Time for all Methods] I --> J[Step 10 Calculate Torque and Verify with Formulas] ``` > [!TIP] > **Lab Practical Tip:** Always connect the **voltmeter across the line** and **ammeter in series with the line**, never across a motor phase winding. For the autotransformer method, use a **clip-on ammeter** on the motor terminal leads for safe measurement of starting current. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **"Why is a starter necessary for a 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor? List any three starting methods."** #### Model Answer (3 marks — approx. 80–100 words): A starter is necessary because at the moment of switching on, an induction motor draws a very high **inrush (starting) current of 5 to 7 times** its rated full-load current, while the rotor is at standstill ($s = 1$). This high current causes **excessive voltage drop** in the supply network, **overheating of stator windings**, and **mechanical stress** on the shaft, coupling and load. It may also trip the upstream protective devices. Three common starting methods are: 1. **Direct-On-Line (DOL)** starting 2. **Star-Delta (Y–Δ)** reduced-voltage starting 3. **Autotransformer** reduced-voltage starting **(Mark distribution: 1 mark for reason + 1 mark for problem + 1 mark for listing 3 methods)** --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **"Compare star-delta and autotransformer starting methods on the basis of: (i) voltage reduction factor, (ii) starting torque, and (iii) cost."** #### Model Answer (3 marks — table format preferred): | Parameter | Star-Delta Starter | Autotransformer Starter | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | (i) Voltage reduction factor | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577$ | Tap selectable: 0.5, 0.65 or 0.80 | | (ii) Starting torque | $\dfrac{1}{3} \times T_{DOL}$ (fixed) | $x^2 \times T_{DOL}$ (adjustable) | | (iii) Cost | Low (only contactors) | High (autotransformer adds cost) | **(Mark distribution: 1 mark each point, table format earns full credit)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Internal Choice]` **(a)** With the help of a neat power circuit diagram, explain the **star-delta starting method** of a 3-phase induction motor. Describe the role of each contactor and the timer in the operation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, **400 V**, **50 Hz**, 4-pole squirrel-cage induction motor has a **standstill impedance per phase of $(3 + j4) \, \Omega$**. Calculate: - (i) The DOL starting current (line value) - (ii) The starting current in star mode - (iii) The starting torque in star mode, if DOL starting torque is **120 Nm** - (iv) Comment on whether the motor can start against a load torque of **35 Nm** in star mode. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A: ### Part (a) — Star-Delta Starter Explanation (7 marks) **Hardware Components and Their Roles** (2 marks): | Component | Symbol | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Main Contactor | K1 | Connects the 3-phase supply to motor terminals U1, V1, W1 | | Star Contactor | K2 | Shorts U2, V2, W2 to form star point during starting | | Delta Contactor | K3 | Closes to connect windings in delta during running | | Timer Relay | T | Energises K2 initially, then after preset time transfers to K3 | | Overload Relay | OLR | Trips K1 in case of sustained overcurrent | **Operating Sequence** (3 marks): 1. **At rest:** All contactors open. Motor isolated from supply. 2. **Press START:** Timer is energised. Timer immediately closes **K1 (main)** and **K2 (star)**. Motor windings receive $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ per phase. Motor begins to accelerate. 3. **Acceleration phase:** Rotor accelerates to ~70–80 % of $N_s$. Timer counts down (typically 3–10 seconds). 4. **Transition:** Timer opens **K2** (star disconnected) and closes **K3** (delta connected). This is the **open transition** — a brief supply interruption occurs. 5. **Run condition:** K1 and K3 remain closed. K2 stays open. Motor now runs in delta at full line voltage per phase. **Diagram** (2 marks): A neat labelled diagram showing 3-phase supply → MCB → K1 → (U1, V1, W1); motor windings with terminals; K2 shorting U2-V2-W2; K3 forming delta link; timer controlling the switching sequence. --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** - Line voltage: $V_L = 400$ V - Standstill impedance: $Z_{ph} = 3 + j4 \, \Omega$ - Magnitude: $\vert Z_{ph} \vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \, \Omega$ - DOL starting torque: $T_{DOL} = 120$ Nm - Load torque at start: $T_L = 35$ Nm --- **(i) DOL Starting Current (Line value)** (2 marks): Phase voltage in delta = $V_L = 400$ V $$ \begin{aligned} I_{ph,DOL} &= \frac{V_L}{\vert Z_{ph} \vert} = \frac{400}{5} = 80 \, A \\[6pt] I_{L,DOL} &= \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph,DOL} = \sqrt{3} \times 80 = 138.56 \, A \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{I_{L,DOL} \approx 138.56 \, A} $$ --- **(ii) Starting Current in Star Mode** (2 marks): Phase voltage in star = $V_L/\sqrt{3} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V $$ \begin{aligned} I_{ph,Y} &= \frac{V_{ph,Y}}{\vert Z_{ph} \vert} = \frac{230.94}{5} = 46.19 \, A \\[6pt] I_{L,Y} &= I_{ph,Y} = 46.19 \, A \end{aligned} $$ (Alternatively: $I_{L,Y} = I_{L,DOL} / 3 = 138.56 / 3 = 46.19$ A ✓) $$ \boxed{I_{L,Y} \approx 46.19 \, A} $$ --- **(iii) Starting Torque in Star Mode** (2 marks): $$ \begin{aligned} T_Y &= \frac{1}{3} \times T_{DOL} = \frac{1}{3} \times 120 \\[6pt] \therefore T_Y &= 40 \, Nm \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{T_Y = 40 \, Nm} $$ --- **(iv) Can the Motor Start?** (1 mark): Compare: $T_{start} = T_Y = 40$ Nm vs $T_{load} = 35$ Nm Since $T_{start} = 40$ Nm **>** $T_{load} = 35$ Nm, the motor **will start** and accelerate to operating speed. $$ \boxed{\text{Yes, the motor will start since } T_{start} > T_{load}} $$ --- ### QUESTION B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module Internal Choice]` **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **autotransformer starting method** of a 3-phase induction motor with **65 % tap**. Derive the expressions for **starting current drawn from supply** and **starting torque** in terms of DOL values and tap ratio $x$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, **15 HP**, **400 V**, **50 Hz** induction motor has a **full-load current of 25 A** and **full-load torque of 60 Nm**. The DOL starting current is **6 times** the full-load current. If the motor is started using an autotransformer with **75 % tap**, calculate: - (i) Starting current drawn by the motor winding - (ii) Starting current drawn from the supply - (iii) Starting torque developed - (iv) Starting torque as a percentage of full-load torque **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B: ### Part (a) — Autotransformer Starter Explanation (7 marks) **Principle** (2 marks): A 3-phase autotransformer with multiple taps (50 %, 65 %, 80 %) is connected between the supply and the motor. At the moment of starting, the motor is supplied through the selected tap, giving a reduced voltage $V_{motor} = x \cdot V_L$. After the rotor accelerates, the autotransformer is bypassed via a contactor and the motor is connected directly to the full supply voltage. **Circuit Description** (3 marks): - **Isolator/MCCB:** Main isolation switch. - **Main Contactor K1:** Connects the supply to the primary of the autotransformer and to the motor common neutral point. - **Tap Contactor K2:** Connects the selected tap to the motor terminals (closed during START). - **Bypass Contactor K3:** Directly connects supply to motor terminals (closed during RUN). - **Timer Relay T:** Controls transition from K2 to K3 after preset time. - **Overload Relay OLR:** Protects motor from sustained overcurrent. **Sequence of Operation:** 1. Press START → K1 closes → K2 closes (motor receives $x V_L$) 2. Motor accelerates under reduced voltage 3. After timer preset → K2 opens → K3 closes (motor receives $V_L$ directly) 4. Motor runs at full voltage **Derivations** (2 marks): Starting current drawn by the motor: $$ I_{motor} = x \cdot I_{ph,DOL} $$ Starting current drawn from supply (from power balance): $$ I_{line,auto} = x^2 \cdot I_{L,DOL} $$ Starting torque: $$ T_{auto} = x^2 \cdot T_{DOL} $$ **Diagram** (essential): A neat labelled diagram showing 3-phase supply → autotransformer (with 3 taps) → motor terminals; contactors K1, K2, K3; timer; OLR. --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** - Full-load current: $I_{FL} = 25$ A - DOL starting current (line): $I_{L,DOL} = 6 \times I_{FL} = 6 \times 25 = 150$ A - Full-load torque: $T_{FL} = 60$ Nm - Tap ratio: $x = 75 \% = 0.75$ **DOL starting torque** (assume standard: $T_{DOL} \approx 1.5 \times T_{FL}$): $$ T_{DOL} = 1.5 \times 60 = 90 \, Nm $$ (If a different value is given in problem statement, use that value.) --- **(i) Starting Current Drawn by Motor Winding** (2 marks): The current through the motor winding at reduced voltage $V_{motor} = 0.75 \times V_L$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{motor} &= x \cdot I_{ph,DOL} \\[6pt] &= 0.75 \cdot \frac{I_{L,DOL}}{\sqrt{3}} \\[6pt] &= 0.75 \cdot \frac{150}{\sqrt{3}} \\[6pt] &= 0.75 \cdot 86.60 \\[6pt] &= 64.95 \, A \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{I_{motor} \approx 64.95 \, A} $$ --- **(ii) Starting Current Drawn from Supply** (2 marks): Using power balance: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{line,auto} &= x^2 \cdot I_{L,DOL} \\[6pt] &= (0.75)^2 \cdot 150 \\[6pt] &= 0.5625 \cdot 150 \\[6pt] &= 84.375 \, A \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{I_{line,auto} \approx 84.38 \, A} $$ --- **(iii) Starting Torque Developed** (1 mark): $$ \begin{aligned} T_{auto} &= x^2 \cdot T_{DOL} \\[6pt] &= 0.5625 \cdot 90 \\[6pt] &= 50.625 \, Nm \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{auto} \approx 50.63 \, Nm} $$ --- **(iv) Starting Torque as Percentage of Full-Load Torque** (2 marks): $$ \begin{aligned} \% T &= \frac{T_{auto}}{T_{FL}} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{50.625}{60} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 84.375 \% \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\frac{T_{auto}}{T_{FL}} \approx 84.38\%} $$ **Conclusion:** The autotransformer with 75 % tap reduces the starting current to **56.25 %** of DOL and provides starting torque of **84.38 %** of full-load torque — a much better starting performance than star-delta. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing line and phase currents** — In star mode, $I_L = I_{ph}$; in delta mode, $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$. Many students wrongly interchange these. **Memorise the table.** > > 2. **Forgetting the square factor in autotransformer** — The motor current reduces by factor $x$, but the **line current reduces by factor $x^2$**. Marks lost if this distinction is missed. > > 3. **Using 230 V instead of 400 V** — In Kerala, standard 3-phase supply is **400 V (line-to-line)**, not 415 V. Some textbooks use 415 V; **read the problem statement carefully**. > > 4. **Wrong tap ratio definition** — $x$ = fraction of supply voltage applied to motor (e.g., 0.65 for 65 % tap), **not** the autotransformer turns ratio $K$. Do not invert. > > 5. **Not stating the assumption of ideal autotransformer** — In derivations, mention "ideal autotransformer" or "neglecting magnetising current" to get full credit. > > 6. **Missing diagram** — In 7-mark questions, a **labelled circuit diagram is mandatory**. Without it, up to **2 marks** are deducted. > > 7. **Skipping units** — Always mention **A, V, Nm, RPM** at the end of every numerical answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **Starting current** in DOL = **5 to 7 × $I_{FL}$** - [x] **Starting torque** in DOL = **1.0 to 2.0 × $T_{FL}$** - [x] **Star-Delta:** $I_{star} = \dfrac{I_{DOL}}{3}$ and $T_{star} = \dfrac{T_{DOL}}{3}$ - [x] **Autotransformer:** $I_{line} = x^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$ and $T_{auto} = x^2 \cdot T_{DOL}$ - [x] **Star-Delta** is equivalent to autotransformer with **$x = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$** - [x] Star-Delta requires **6 accessible motor terminals**; autotransformer needs only **3 terminals** - [x] Autotransformer is **more expensive** but offers **adjustable** starting performance - [x] Star-Delta causes **open transition** (brief supply break); autotransformer can be **closed transition** - [x] Synchronous speed formula: $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ - [x] Star mode: $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ and $I_L = I_{ph}$ - [x] Delta mode: $V_{ph} = V_L$ and $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$ - [x] Lab observation table must include: **voltage, line current, motor current, speed, torque, time** - [x] Graphs to plot: **Speed vs Time**, **Current vs Time**, **Torque vs Slip** - [x] Always connect **overload relay** in series with main contactor - [x] Timer setting for star-delta: typically **3 to 10 seconds**, adjusted by trial ### One-Line Memory Aids for KTU Viva | Mnemonic | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **"Star is one-third, both current and torque"** | $I_Y = I_{DOL}/3$, $T_Y = T_{DOL}/3$ | | **"Tap squared for transformer"** | $I_{line} = x^2 I_{DOL}$ | | **"Motor current, tap to first power"** | $I_{motor} = x \cdot I_{DOL}$ | | **"Voltage squared, torque is shared"** | $T \propto V^2$ always | | **"Delta line, sqrt-three, line-to-phase ratio"** | $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$ in delta | > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Strategy (Before KTU Exam):** > 1. Revise **Section 2.3 Table 2.1** — it contains every formula you need. > 2. Solve the **Section 3.5 worked numerical** yourself on a paper without looking. > 3. Draw the **switching sequence diagram** (Section 4.3) from memory. > 4. Memorise the **valuation warning list** to avoid losing marks unnecessarily. > 5. Practise the **comparison table** (Section 2.3 Table 2.2) — it appears in almost every KTU paper. ---

topics.title: Load test on single phase transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUL607 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND MECHATRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Load test on single phase transformer ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Load Test on a Single-Phase Transformer:** A direct-loading laboratory experiment in which a single-phase transformer is operated at its rated voltage and frequency while supplying a variable, purely resistive (or mixed) load. The primary and secondary side meters (voltmeter, ammeter, wattmeter) are observed at incremental load fractions, and the **efficiency (η)** and **voltage regulation (VR%)** are computed as functions of output current. This test is the *gold-standard* for determining the steady-state thermal and electrical performance of a transformer under real operating conditions. The load test directly gives: - **Efficiency (η)** at any load - **Voltage Regulation (VR%)** at any load - **Nature of the load (lagging, leading, unity, or zero)** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the transformer like a **water pump delivering water through a pipe**: - The **primary winding** is the electric input "pumping energy" in. - The **secondary winding** is the output "tap" delivering energy to your appliance (load). - The **load (rheostat / lamp bank)** is like opening the tap — the wider you open it (lower resistance), the more current flows, and the heavier the pump has to work. When you gradually "open the tap" from 0% to 100% and beyond, you notice two practical things in a real pump (and in a transformer): 1. The **pressure at the tap drops slightly** (in electrical terms: secondary voltage falls — this is *voltage regulation*). 2. **More water flows out, but not all energy pumped in comes out** because of friction (in electrical terms: copper losses + iron losses — this is *efficiency*). > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constant / Standard Metric** > The rated efficiency of small single-phase transformers used in KTU labs typically lies in the range **85% – 95%**. Voltage regulation is usually kept **below 5%** for well-designed units. ### 1.3 Why This Test is Taught in the Lab - It is the **only test that simultaneously reveals efficiency and regulation**. - It is a *direct* test — no indirect approximations. - It validates the equivalent circuit parameters obtained from OC and SC tests. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The load test works on the principle of **direct loading**. The transformer is supplied at rated primary voltage (via an autotransformer/Variac) and the secondary is connected to a variable resistive load (lamp load, resistive rheostat, or water-load). **Step 1 — No-Load Condition** - The secondary load is open (switch open). - Read secondary voltage = **V₂(NL)** → this is the nominal voltage. - Read no-load primary current and no-load input power (W₀). - W₀ represents the **iron (core) losses** since there are no copper losses on the secondary. **Step 2 — Loaded Condition (Incremental Loading)** - Switch ON the load in steps (e.g., 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, 125% of rated current). - For each step, record: - Primary voltmeter reading V₁ - Primary ammeter reading I₁ - Primary wattmeter reading W₁ (input power) - Secondary voltmeter reading V₂ - Secondary ammeter reading I₂ (load current) **Step 3 — Computational Phase** - Compute output power: **W₂ = V₂ × I₂** (assuming unity power factor resistive load). - Compute efficiency: **η = (W₂ / W₁) × 100%**. - Compute regulation: **VR% = ((V₂(NL) − V₂(FL)) / V₂(NL)) × 100%**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Output Power | $W_2 = V_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2$ | Watts (W) | For pure resistive load, $\cos\phi_2 = 1$ | | 2 | Input Power | $W_1 = V_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot \cos\phi_1$ | Watts (W) | Read directly from wattmeter | | 3 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100\%$ | % | Always $\leq 100\%$ | | 4 | Voltage Regulation | $VR\% = \dfrac{V_{2(NL)} - V_{2(FL)}}{V_{2(NL)}} \times 100\%$ | % | Sign: +ve for lagging or unity load | | 5 | Load Fraction | $x = \dfrac{I_2}{I_{2(\text{rated})}}$ | dimensionless | Used for plotting curves | | 6 | Copper Losses (per phase equivalent) | $P_{cu} = W_1 - W_2 - P_i$ | W | $P_i$ ≈ no-load loss | > [!NOTE] > For a single-phase transformer with purely resistive (lamp) loading, $\cos\phi_2 = 1$, so $W_2 = V_2 \cdot I_2$. This simplification is what makes the lab computation clean for KTU viva questions. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Transformer manufacturers** perform load tests on every unit before dispatch to certify rated kVA and efficiency. - **Electrical power utilities** verify the nameplate performance of distribution transformers in field acceptance tests. - **Design validation** — the measured regulation is cross-checked with the value predicted from equivalent circuit parameters (Z₀₁, R₀₁, X₀₁) found in OC/SC tests. - **Fault diagnosis** — abnormal voltage drop across loading may indicate winding short, loose tap connections, or core saturation. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why Efficiency has a Maximum at a Particular Load The efficiency of a transformer is defined as: $$\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}} = \frac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 + P_i + P_{cu}}$$ Let the load fraction be $x$, where $x = \dfrac{I_2}{I_{2(\text{rated})}}$, and the full-load copper loss be $P_{cu(\text{FL})}$. **Step 1 — Express copper loss at load fraction x** $$P_{cu}(x) = x^2 \cdot P_{cu(\text{FL})}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into efficiency expression** $$\eta(x) = \frac{x \cdot V_2 I_{2(\text{rated})} \cos\phi_2}{x \cdot V_2 I_{2(\text{rated})} \cos\phi_2 + P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu(\text{FL})}}$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate $\eta$ with respect to x and set to zero** For maximum efficiency, the standard condition is well known: $$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_i = x^2 \cdot P_{cu(\text{FL})}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the load fraction at maximum efficiency** $$x_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu(\text{FL})}}}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation** $$\boxed{\,x_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{cu(\text{FL})}}}\,}$$ This is **why a transformer is most efficient at that particular load where iron losses equal copper losses** — a critical viva question in KTU labs. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Performance at Different Load Levels | Load Fraction $x$ | $P_{cu}(x)$ | Iron Loss $P_i$ | Total Loss | Efficiency $\eta$ | Voltage Regulation $VR\%$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0.00 (No-load) | 0 | $P_i$ | $P_i$ | 0% | 0% | | 0.25 | $0.0625\,P_{cu(\text{FL})}$ | $P_i$ | $P_i + 0.0625\,P_{cu(\text{FL})}$ | Low | Very Low | | 0.50 | $0.25\,P_{cu(\text{FL})}$ | $P_i$ | $P_i + 0.25\,P_{cu(\text{FL})}$ | Rising | Low | | 1.00 (Full-load) | $P_{cu(\text{FL})}$ | $P_i$ | $P_i + P_{cu(\text{FL})}$ | Rated $\eta$ | Rated $VR\%$ | | 1.25 (Overload) | $1.5625\,P_{cu(\text{FL})}$ | $P_i$ | $P_i + 1.5625\,P_{cu(\text{FL})}$ | Falling | High | ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU-Style Question) > **Given:** A 1 kVA, 230/115 V single-phase transformer. From the load test on the LV side (115 V winding loaded), the following readings are tabulated. Compute efficiency and regulation. | S.No. | $I_2$ (A) | $V_2$ (V) | $V_1$ (V) | $I_1$ (A) | $W_1$ (W) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 0.0 | 115.0 | 230 | 0.40 | 8.0 | | 2 | 2.0 | 113.5 | 230 | 1.20 | 245 | | 3 | 4.0 | 112.0 | 230 | 2.20 | 478 | | 4 | 6.0 | 110.5 | 230 | 3.30 | 705 | | 5 | 8.0 | 109.0 | 230 | 4.40 | 925 | | 6 | 8.7 (rated) | 108.5 | 230 | 4.75 | 1005 | **Step 1 — Output power at each loading (resistive load, $\cos\phi_2 = 1$)** $$W_2 = V_2 \cdot I_2$$ For S.No. 4: $\quad W_2 = 110.5 \times 6.0 = 663.0\,\text{W}$ **Step 2 — Efficiency at S.No. 4** $$\eta = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100\% = \frac{663.0}{705.0} \times 100\% \approx 94.04\%$$ **Step 3 — Voltage Regulation (compare rated current 8.7 A to no-load 115 V)** $$VR\% = \frac{115.0 - 108.5}{115.0} \times 100\% \approx 5.65\%$$ This example demonstrates a typical efficiency band of **93% – 95%** and regulation **≈ 5%**, both characteristic of a well-designed 1 kVA lab transformer. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Circuit Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) The circuit is rendered as a functional flow diagram (Mermaid-compliant, avoiding reserved keywords): ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Supply Mains
230 V 50 Hz] --> B[Auto Transformer
Variac 0 to 270 V] B --> C[Voltmeter V1
Primary Side] C --> D[Ammeter A1
Primary Side] D --> E[Wattmeter W1
Primary Side] E --> F[Transformer
Primary Winding] F --> G[Transformer
Secondary Winding] G --> H[Voltmeter V2
Secondary Side] H --> I[Ammeter A2
Secondary Side] I --> J[Load Switch
SPST] J --> K[Lamp Bank or
Rheostatic Load] K --> L[Common Earth
Neutral Return] L --> G style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600 style F fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style G fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style K fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000 ``` ### 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Step 1: Check nameplate ratings
of transformer] --> P2[Step 2: Make connections as
per circuit diagram] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Keep Variac at zero
and load switch OPEN] P3 --> P4[Step 4: Switch ON supply
and slowly increase Variac
to rated primary voltage] P4 --> P5[Step 5: Record no-load readings
V1, V2 NL, I1, W1] P5 --> P6[Step 6: Close load switch and
load in steps of 25 percent] P6 --> P7[Step 7: For each load,
record V1, I1, W1, V2, I2] P7 --> P8[Step 8: Repeat until
125 percent overload] P8 --> P9[Step 9: Reduce Variac to zero
then switch OFF supply] P9 --> P10[Step 10: Compute efficiency
and regulation and plot graphs] ``` ### 4.3 Performance Curves (Schematic Description) Two characteristic curves are drawn using the tabulated values: ```mermaid graph LR subgraph OutputCurrent[Load Current I2 in Amps] direction LR A1[0] --> A2[2] --> A3[4] --> A4[6] --> A5[8] --> A6[8.7] end subgraph EffCurve[Efficiency Curve - Rises then falls] E1[0 percent] --> E2[84 percent] --> E3[92 percent] --> E4[94 percent] --> E5[94.4 percent] --> E6[94.0 percent] end subgraph RegCurve[Regulation Curve - Monotonically rises] R1[0 percent] --> R2[1.3 percent] --> R3[2.6 percent] --> R4[3.9 percent] --> R5[5.2 percent] --> R6[5.65 percent] end ``` **X-axis (common):** Load current $I_2$ (or load fraction $x$). **Y-axis (left):** Efficiency $\eta$ in %. **Y-axis (right):** Voltage regulation $VR\%$ in %. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define voltage regulation of a transformer. Why is it an important performance indicator?* **Model Answer:** Voltage regulation of a transformer is defined as the change in secondary terminal voltage from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of the no-load voltage, with primary voltage held constant at rated value. $$VR\% = \frac{V_{2(\text{NL})} - V_{2(\text{FL})}}{V_{2(\text{NL})}} \times 100\%$$ **Importance:** It indicates how well the transformer maintains a constant output voltage under varying load. A low regulation means the transformer is suitable for supplying loads that are sensitive to voltage fluctuations (e.g., electronics, motors). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question]** *List the various losses occurring in a transformer and state how each varies with load.* **Model Answer:** The losses in a transformer are: | Loss Type | Nature | Variation with Load | |---|---|---| | Iron / Core loss ($P_i$) | Hysteresis + Eddy current | **Constant** (independent of load) | | Copper loss ($P_{cu}$) | $I^2R$ in windings | **Proportional to (load)²** | | Stray load loss | Leakage flux in structural parts | Proportional to (load)² | | Dielectric loss | In insulation at high voltage | Negligible at low voltage | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Question (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Load Test Performance Analysis)* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** *During a load test on a 2 kVA, 230 V / 115 V single-phase transformer, the following readings were observed on the LV side:* | Load | $I_2$ (A) | $V_2$ (V) | $W_1$ (W) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | No-load | 0 | 115.0 | 22 | | 1/4 | 4.35 | 114.0 | 540 | | 1/2 | 8.70 | 113.0 | 1050 | | 3/4 | 13.05 | 112.0 | 1560 | | Full | 17.40 | 110.8 | 2070 | **(a)** Calculate the **efficiency at each load** and the **voltage regulation at full load**. **(b)** Plot the **efficiency vs load current** and **regulation vs load current** curves, and comment on the results. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Efficiency at each load (resistive, $\cos\phi_2 = 1$)** **Step 1** — Compute output power at each load using $W_2 = V_2 \cdot I_2$: $$\begin{aligned} W_2\,(1/4) &= 114.0 \times 4.35 = 495.9\,\text{W} \\ W_2\,(1/2) &= 113.0 \times 8.70 = 983.1\,\text{W} \\ W_2\,(3/4) &= 112.0 \times 13.05 = 1461.6\,\text{W} \\ W_2\,(1/1) &= 110.8 \times 17.40 = 1927.9\,\text{W} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2** — Apply $\eta = (W_2 / W_1) \times 100\%$: $$\begin{aligned} \eta\,(1/4) &= \frac{495.9}{540} \times 100 = 91.83\% \\ \eta\,(1/2) &= \frac{983.1}{1050} \times 100 = 93.63\% \\ \eta\,(3/4) &= \frac{1461.6}{1560} \times 100 = 93.69\% \\ \eta\,(1/1) &= \frac{1927.9}{2070} \times 100 = 93.13\% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3** — Voltage regulation at full load: $$VR\% = \frac{V_{2(\text{NL})} - V_{2(\text{FL})}}{V_{2(\text{NL})}} \times 100\% = \frac{115.0 - 110.8}{115.0} \times 100\% \approx 3.65\%$$ **(b) Plots and Comments** Plot $I_2$ along X-axis, $\eta$ and $VR\%$ along Y-axes. Observations: - **Efficiency curve** rises rapidly, peaks near 3/4 load (≈ 93.7%), then slightly drops at full load. This confirms that maximum efficiency occurs where iron loss ≈ copper loss (slightly below full load). - **Regulation curve** rises almost linearly with load current — typical of a resistive-load test. - The transformer operates satisfactorily with **η > 93%** and **VR < 4%**, well within standard design limits. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Efficiency Condition + Predictions)* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question]** **(a)** Derive the condition for **maximum efficiency** of a transformer. From a no-load test, the iron loss is found to be **80 W** and from the short-circuit test (at full-load current) the copper loss is **120 W**. **(b)** At what load (in kVA) does the transformer operate at maximum efficiency if its rating is **5 kVA**? Also calculate the **maximum efficiency at unity power factor**. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (re-stated for completeness)** $$\eta = \frac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu(\text{FL})}}$$ For $\dfrac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$: $$\boxed{\,x_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu(\text{FL})}}}\,}$$ **Substitute numerical values:** $$x_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{80}{120}} = \sqrt{0.6667} \approx 0.8165$$ **Load at maximum efficiency:** $$S_{\max} = x_{\max} \times S_{\text{rated}} = 0.8165 \times 5 = 4.0825\,\text{kVA}$$ **(b) Maximum efficiency at unity power factor** At $x = 0.8165$, copper loss = $x^2 \cdot P_{cu(\text{FL})} = (0.8165)^2 \times 120 = 80\,\text{W}$ (which equals $P_i$, as expected). Output power at maximum efficiency: $$W_{2(\text{out})} = S_{\max} \times 1000 \times \cos\phi = 4082.5 \times 1 = 4082.5\,\text{W}$$ Total losses = $P_i + P_{cu} = 80 + 80 = 160\,\text{W}$ $$\eta_{\max} = \frac{4082.5}{4082.5 + 160} \times 100\% \approx 96.22\%$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > - **Forgetting the no-load voltage** when computing regulation — many students use the rated voltage instead of the actual measured $V_{2(\text{NL})}$ and lose **2–3 marks**. > - **Mixing up primary and secondary** voltmeter readings. Always remember: regulation is computed on the **secondary side**. > - **Power factor omission** — for a pure resistive lamp load, state $\cos\phi = 1$ explicitly in your answer. > - **Graph axes labels and units** — without proper labelling, full marks are not awarded for the plot. > - **Sign convention** — if loading uses an inductive load, regulation is positive; capacitive load gives negative regulation. KTU typically tests resistive loads, but be aware. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Load test** is a *direct method* to find efficiency and regulation simultaneously. - ✅ Always keep **primary voltage constant** at rated value throughout the test. - ✅ **No-load voltage** $V_{2(\text{NL})}$ is the reference for regulation calculation. - ✅ For resistive (lamp) loads, $\cos\phi_2 = 1$, simplifying $W_2 = V_2 \cdot I_2$. - ✅ **Efficiency maximum** occurs where $P_i = P_{cu}(x)$ — never at no-load or short-circuit. - ✅ Always plot **two graphs**: $\eta$ vs $I_2$ and $VR\%$ vs $I_2$ on the same X-axis. - ✅ Record at least **5 to 6 readings** from no-load to 125% rated load. - ✅ Always **reduce Variac to zero** before switching OFF supply — to avoid inrush. - ✅ Use **proper rating meters** — wattmeter voltage coil should match the side where it is connected. - ✅ Always include **fuses / MCBs** in both primary and secondary circuits — KTU strictly checks this in viva. - ✅ **Iron loss is constant**, copper loss varies as the **square of load current**. - ✅ **Voltage regulation** is positive for lagging/unity loads and can be **negative** for leading (capacitive) loads. - ✅ The KTU lab report should include **nameplate details, circuit diagram, observation table, sample calculation, graphs, and result with inference**. ---

topics.title: OC and SC test on single phase transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL607 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND MECHATRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** OC and SC test on single phase transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Open Circuit (OC) Test** and **Short Circuit (SC) Test** on a single-phase transformer constitute the two most fundamental **indirect (non-loading) test methods** prescribed by KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for the determination of equivalent circuit parameters and performance characteristics of a single-phase transformer without actually loading it to its full rated capacity. Together, these tests yield the **core (iron) losses, no-load current, equivalent resistance, equivalent reactance, percentage voltage regulation, and efficiency** of the transformer at any desired load and power factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMUL607 - Module 2):**
> *"Determination of equivalent circuit parameters of a single-phase transformer by OC and SC test and computation of efficiency and regulation."*

| Test | Side Energized | Side Kept Open/Shorted | Primary Quantities Measured |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OC Test** | LV (Low Voltage) | HV Open | $V_0,\ I_0,\ W_0$ |
| **SC Test** | HV (High Voltage) | LV Shorted | $V_{sc},\ I_{sc},\ W_{sc}$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to know how healthy a person is without putting them under extreme physical stress (like running a marathon). Instead, you:
- Make them sit calmly on a chair and measure their resting heartbeat, breathing, and basal metabolic rate → this is the **OC Test** (transformer is idle; it still draws a small magnetizing current and consumes iron losses).
- Make them walk briskly on a treadmill with a small load and measure how hard their heart and lungs work → this is the **SC Test** (transformer carries full-load current with a small applied voltage, revealing copper losses).

In both cases, we extract the **internal "health parameters"** of the subject (transformer) without subjecting it to full destructive load. The OC test reveals the **magnetic circuit health** (iron core, magnetizing reactance), and the SC test reveals the **electrical conductor health** (winding resistance and leakage reactance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two tests and not one?**
> A single test cannot separate iron losses (which depend on voltage) from copper losses (which depend on current). Voltage is high in OC (full V) and low in SC (~5-10% V). Current is low in OC and full in SC. This orthogonal behavior lets us isolate the two loss components.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Rated Frequency:** $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$ (Indian standard, per IS 2026)
- **Permissible SC Test Voltage:** Typically **5% to 10%** of rated voltage
- **No-Load Current:** Typically **2% to 10%** of full-load current
- **Power Factor on OC:** Very low, lagging ($\approx 0.2$ to $0.3$)
- **Power Factor on SC:** Higher, lagging ($\approx 0.5$ to $0.7$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Open Circuit (OC) Test — Step-by-Step Logic

**Setup:** The LV winding is connected to a variable AC supply through an **autotransformer (variac)**. The HV winding is left **open-circuited**. Instruments placed on the LV (primary) side are:
- **Voltmeter (V)** — across supply
- **Ammeter (A)** — in series with the winding
- **Wattmeter (W)** — current coil in series, pressure coil across the supply

**Procedure:**
1. Apply **rated voltage** $V_0$ (at rated frequency) to the LV winding using the variac.
2. The HV side is open, so there is no load current on the secondary.
3. The small current $I_0$ drawn from the supply has two purposes: to set up the working flux in the core, and to supply the iron losses.
4. Read the three instruments: $V_0$ (voltmeter), $I_0$ (ammeter), $W_0$ (wattmeter).

**Why is the HV side kept open?**
Because at no-load, secondary current $I_2 = 0$. Whatever small current flows in the primary is solely the **no-load current** $I_0$, which represents the magnetizing branch. If we had shorted the HV side, we would be drawing short-circuit current, not no-load current.

**Why LV side is energized (and not HV)?**
For convenience and safety — the rated voltage on the LV side is low (e.g., 230 V) and easier to apply with laboratory variacs and meters. The HV side's nominal voltage (e.g., 11 kV) would be dangerous and require specialized, costly meters.

> [!NOTE]
> The wattmeter reading $W_0$ at no-load equals the **sum of core (iron) loss** $P_i$ and a tiny **no-load copper loss** $I_0^2 R_1$. Since $I_0$ is very small, $I_0^2 R_1 \ll P_i$, so $W_0 \approx P_i$ (iron loss).

### 2.1.1 Decomposition of No-Load Current

The no-load current $I_0$ can be resolved into two perpendicular components:
- **Working (Iron-loss) component** $I_w$ — in phase with $V_0$, supplies iron loss
- **Magnetizing component** $I_\mu$ — 90° lagging $V_0$, sets up the core flux

$$I_w = I_0 \cos \phi_0 \quad \text{(in-phase with voltage)}$$

$$I_\mu = I_0 \sin \phi_0 \quad \text{(quadrature to voltage)}$$

### 2.1.2 No-Load Power Factor

$$\cos \phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{V_0 \, I_0}$$

### 2.1.3 Equivalent Magnetizing Branch Parameters (referred to LV side)

$$R_0 = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{V_0^2}{W_0}$$

$$X_0 = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu}$$

## 2.2 The Short Circuit (SC) Test — Step-by-Step Logic

**Setup:** The HV winding is connected to a variac. The LV winding is **short-circuited through a thick copper bar or ammeter**. Instruments on the HV side measure $V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $W_{sc}$.

**Procedure:**
1. With the LV shorted, gradually increase the HV-side voltage from zero using the variac.
2. Watch the ammeter; raise the voltage until the **ammeter reads the rated full-load current** of the HV side.
3. Read all three instruments: $V_{sc}$ (typically 5-10% of rated), $I_{sc}$ (rated), $W_{sc}$.

**Why such a low applied voltage?**
A shorted transformer behaves like a small impedance (only the winding resistance and leakage reactance). If we applied full rated voltage, the current would be enormous (perhaps 10-20 times rated), which would damage the windings. Hence, we apply just enough voltage to circulate **rated full-load current** in the windings.

**Why is the LV side shorted (and not HV)?**
Two reasons: (1) the SC test current is rated full-load current, which on the HV side is much smaller and easier to handle with standard meters; (2) the variac available in the lab is usually low-voltage high-current, matching the LV side's shorted condition more conveniently when HV is energized. In **KTU lab practice**, the HV side is energized because the meters used are typically rated for the LV side ranges and the shorted LV side ensures low-voltage operation overall.

> [!NOTE]
> The wattmeter reading $W_{sc}$ equals the **full-load copper loss** $P_{cu}$ because at SC, the applied voltage is small, so iron losses (which depend on $V^2$) are negligible: $W_{sc} \approx P_{cu,FL}$.

### 2.2.1 Equivalent Impedance (referred to HV side)

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$$

$$R_{01} = \frac{W_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$$

These are the total equivalent series parameters referred to the HV side. To refer to the LV side, divide by $K^2$, where $K$ is the turns ratio.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Test Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | No-load power factor | $\cos \phi_0 = \dfrac{W_0}{V_0 \, I_0}$ | dimensionless | OC |
| 2 | Iron-loss component | $I_w = I_0 \cos \phi_0$ | A | OC |
| 3 | Magnetizing component | $I_\mu = I_0 \sin \phi_0$ | A | OC |
| 4 | Iron loss | $P_i = W_0$ | W | OC |
| 5 | Core-loss resistance | $R_0 = \dfrac{V_0^2}{W_0}$ | $\Omega$ | OC |
| 6 | Magnetizing reactance | $X_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{I_\mu}$ | $\Omega$ | OC |
| 7 | Equivalent impedance | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$ | $\Omega$ | SC |
| 8 | Equivalent resistance | $R_{01} = \dfrac{W_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}$ | $\Omega$ | SC |
| 9 | Equivalent leakage reactance | $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$ | $\Omega$ | SC |
| 10 | Full-load copper loss | $P_{cu,FL} = W_{sc}$ | W | SC |
| 11 | Efficiency at load fraction $x$, p.f. $\cos \phi$ | $\eta = \dfrac{x \cdot V_2 I_2 \cos \phi}{x \cdot V_2 I_2 \cos \phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,FL}}$ | % (×100) | Derived |
| 12 | Voltage regulation (lagging p.f.) | $\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{I_2 (R_{02}\cos \phi + X_{02}\sin \phi)}{V_2} \times 100$ | % | Derived |
| 13 | Voltage regulation (leading p.f.) | $\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{I_2 (R_{02}\cos \phi - X_{02}\sin \phi)}{V_2} \times 100$ | % | Derived |
| 14 | Turns ratio | $K = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | dimensionless | Nameplate |
| 15 | Referred resistance | $R_{02} = \dfrac{R_{01}}{K^2}$ | $\Omega$ | Conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Caution:** In the table above, $\vert$ notation in formulas has been avoided to prevent markdown table breakage. All absolute value uses $\sqrt{\cdot}$ or direct context.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transformer Manufacturers:** These two tests are mandatory in every factory test report (as per IS 2026 / IEC 60076) before a transformer is dispatched.
- **Condition Monitoring:** Periodic OC-SC tests in service help detect winding degradation, insulation deterioration, and inter-turn shorts.
- **Power System Design:** Engineers use the equivalent parameters to predict voltage drop across transformers feeding loads at various power factors.
- **Loss Optimization:** Modern amorphous-core and CRGO-steel transformers are graded using iron loss values from OC tests.
- **Renewable Integration:** Solar and wind farm step-up transformers undergo OC-SC testing during commissioning to validate nameplate parameters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Efficiency of a Single-Phase Transformer
