**Statement:** Show that the efficiency of a single-phase transformer at any load fraction $x$ and power factor $\cos \phi$ is given by:

$$\eta = \frac{x \cdot S_{rated} \cdot \cos \phi}{x \cdot S_{rated} \cdot \cos \phi + P_i + x^2 \, P_{cu,FL}}$$

where $S_{rated} = V_2 I_2$ is the rated apparent power.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define efficiency as the ratio of output power to input power.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}}$$

The text row justification: Efficiency is fundamentally (useful output) / (total power drawn). The denominator expands output with all losses added because input = output + losses (energy conservation).

**Step 2:** Express the output power at load fraction $x$.

At load fraction $x$, the secondary current is $x \cdot I_{2,FL}$ and the secondary voltage is approximately $V_2$ (neglecting small voltage drop). The output apparent power is $x \cdot V_2 \cdot I_{2,FL}$, and real output power is:

$$P_{out} = x \cdot V_2 \cdot I_{2,FL} \cdot \cos \phi$$

The text row justification: Real power is apparent power times power factor. The factor $x$ appears because at half load, current is halved; at full load, $x = 1$.

**Step 3:** Express the iron loss (constant loss).

Iron loss depends on voltage and frequency, both of which are essentially constant from no-load to full-load. Hence:

$$P_{iron} = P_i = W_0 \quad \text{(from OC test, constant)}$$

**Step 4:** Express the copper loss (variable loss).

Copper loss in a winding is $I^2 R$. At full load, $P_{cu,FL} = I_{FL}^2 R_{01} = W_{sc}$. At load fraction $x$, the current is $x \cdot I_{FL}$, so:

$$P_{cu} = (x \, I_{FL})^2 \cdot R_{01} = x^2 \cdot I_{FL}^2 \cdot R_{01} = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL}$$

The text row justification: Copper loss scales as the **square** of the current. This quadratic dependence is critical and is why the SC test directly gives the full-load copper loss value.

**Step 5:** Substitute into the efficiency equation.

$$\eta = \frac{x \, V_2 \, I_{2,FL} \cos \phi}{x \, V_2 \, I_{2,FL} \cos \phi + P_i + x^2 \, P_{cu,FL}}$$

The text row justification: All three pieces — output, iron loss, and copper loss — are now expressed in terms of measurable OC-SC test quantities and the load variable $x$.

**Step 6:** Define the rated apparent power for compactness.

Let $S = V_2 I_{2,FL}$ (rated VA). Then:

$$\eta = \frac{x \, S \, \cos \phi}{x \, S \, \cos \phi + P_i + x^2 \, P_{cu,FL}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for transformer efficiency. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Regulation (Approximate Method)

**Statement:** Derive the approximate voltage regulation for a single-phase transformer supplying a load at lagging power factor $\cos \phi$.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider the approximate equivalent circuit referred to the secondary side. The primary voltage $V_1$ referred to secondary is $V_1/K = V_2'$. The phasor sum is:

$$V_1' = V_2 + I_2 (R_{02} + j X_{02})$$

The text row justification: The equivalent circuit shows primary voltage (referred) as the vector sum of secondary terminal voltage and the drop across the series impedance.

**Step 2:** Take real components along $V_2$ (we align $V_2$ along the real axis for simplicity):

$$V_1' = V_2 + I_2 R_{02} \cos \phi + I_2 X_{02} \sin \phi \quad \text{(lagging p.f.)}$$

The text row justification: With $V_2$ on the real axis, the current phasor is at angle $-\phi$ (lagging). The horizontal projection of $I_2 R_{02}$ is $I_2 R_{02} \cos \phi$, and the vertical projection of $j I_2 X_{02}$ is $I_2 X_{02} \sin \phi$ (added because the imaginary part of $-jI_2 X_{02}$ is $+I_2 X_{02} \sin\phi$ for lagging loads).

**Step 3:** Voltage regulation is the change in secondary voltage from no-load to full-load, expressed as a fraction of full-load voltage.

$$\%\text{Reg} = \frac{V_1' - V_2}{V_2} \times 100 = \frac{I_2 (R_{02} \cos \phi + X_{02} \sin \phi)}{V_2} \times 100$$

For **leading** power factor, the $X_{02} \sin \phi$ term changes sign:

$$\%\text{Reg}_{\text{leading}} = \frac{I_2 (R_{02} \cos \phi - X_{02} \sin \phi)}{V_2} \times 100$$

The text row justification: For a leading load, the current phasor is at $+\phi$ from $V_2$. The vertical component of $j I_2 X_{02}$ now subtracts, making the regulation smaller and possibly negative (voltage rise). **Q.E.D.**

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 2 kVA, 230 V / 115 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test results:

- **OC Test (LV side energized):** $V_0 = 115\ \text{V}$, $I_0 = 1.2\ \text{A}$, $W_0 = 30\ \text{W}$
- **SC Test (HV side energized):** $V_{sc} = 9.5\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 8.7\ \text{A}$, $W_{sc} = 45\ \text{W}$

Find: (i) Iron loss, (ii) Full-load copper loss, (iii) Efficiency at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging, (iv) Voltage regulation at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging (parameters referred to LV side).

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify the sides.**
Rated current on HV side = $2000 / 230 = 8.7\ \text{A}$ ✓ (matches $I_{sc}$). So SC test was on HV side. Rated current on LV side = $2000 / 115 = 17.4\ \text{A}$. Turns ratio $K = V_1/V_2 = 230/115 = 2$.

**Step 2: Iron loss.**
$P_i = W_0 = 30\ \text{W}$

**Step 3: Full-load copper loss.**
$P_{cu,FL} = W_{sc} = 45\ \text{W}$

**Step 4: No-load power factor.**

$$\cos \phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{V_0 I_0} = \frac{30}{115 \times 1.2} = \frac{30}{138} = 0.2174$$

$$\sin \phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - 0.2174^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.0473} = \sqrt{0.9527} = 0.9761$$

**Step 5: Magnetizing branch parameters (referred to LV side).**

$$I_w = I_0 \cos \phi_0 = 1.2 \times 0.2174 = 0.261\ \text{A}$$

$$I_\mu = I_0 \sin \phi_0 = 1.2 \times 0.9761 = 1.171\ \text{A}$$

$$R_0 = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{115}{0.261} = 440.6\ \Omega$$

$$X_0 = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu} = \frac{115}{1.171} = 98.2\ \Omega$$

**Step 6: Equivalent series parameters referred to HV side (where SC was done).**

$$Z_{01,H} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{9.5}{8.7} = 1.092\ \Omega$$

$$R_{01,H} = \frac{W_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{45}{8.7^2} = \frac{45}{75.69} = 0.5945\ \Omega$$

$$X_{01,H} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2} = \sqrt{1.092^2 - 0.5945^2} = \sqrt{1.1925 - 0.3534} = \sqrt{0.8391} = 0.916\ \Omega$$

**Step 7: Refer to LV side by dividing by $K^2 = 4$.**

$$R_{01,L} = \frac{0.5945}{4} = 0.1486\ \Omega$$

$$X_{01,L} = \frac{0.916}{4} = 0.229\ \Omega$$

**Step 8: Efficiency at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging.**

$$\eta = \frac{2000 \times 0.8}{2000 \times 0.8 + 30 + 45} = \frac{1600}{1600 + 75} = \frac{1600}{1675} = 0.9552 = 95.52\%$$

**Step 9: Voltage regulation at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging (LV side).**
With $\cos \phi = 0.8$, $\sin \phi = 0.6$. LV side rated current $I_2 = 17.4\ \text{A}$, $V_2 = 115\ \text{V}$.

$$\%\text{Reg} = \frac{17.4 \times (0.1486 \times 0.8 + 0.229 \times 0.6)}{115} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{17.4 \times (0.1189 + 0.1374)}{115} \times 100 = \frac{17.4 \times 0.2563}{115} \times 100 = \frac{4.46}{115} \times 100 = 3.88\%$$

**Result Summary:**
- Iron loss = **30 W**
- Full-load copper loss = **45 W**
- Efficiency = **95.52%**
- Voltage regulation = **3.88%**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Complete OC-SC Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply] --> B[Variac Autotransformer]
    B --> C[Test Transformer]
    C --> D[Test Instruments Cluster]
    D --> E[V - Voltmeter]
    D --> F[A - Ammeter]
    D --> G[W - Wattmeter]

    C --> H{Switch Position}
    H -->|OC Test Mode| I[HV Side Open-Circuited]
    H -->|SC Test Mode| J[LV Side Short-Circuited via Ammeter or Copper Bar]

    I --> K[Reads: V0, I0, W0]
    J --> L[Reads: Vsc, Isc, Wsc]

    K --> M[Compute: Iron Loss Pi, No-Load PF, Magnetizing Branch]
    L --> N[Compute: Equivalent Z01, R01, X01, Cu Loss]

    M --> O[Equivalent Circuit Parameters]
    N --> O

    O --> P[Efficiency Calculation]
    O --> Q[Voltage Regulation Calculation]
    O --> R[Performance Report]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid: Phasor Diagram of No-Load Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhasorDiagram [OC Test Phasor Relationships]
        V0["V0 along reference axis"]:::ref
        Iw["Iw in-phase with V0 along real axis"]:::inphase
        Imu["Imu lagging V0 by 90 degrees along negative imaginary axis"]:::quadrature
        I0["I0 phasor = vector sum of Iw and Imu"]:::result
    end

    V0 --> Iw
    V0 --> Imu
    Iw --> I0
    Imu --> I0

    classDef ref fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef inphase fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef quadrature fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef result fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid: Equivalent Circuit of the Transformer (KTU Exam Favorite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V1 Primary Terminal] --> B[R1 Primary Winding Resistance]
    B --> C[X1 Primary Leakage Reactance]
    C --> D[Rc Core Loss Resistance]
    D --> E[Xm Magnetizing Reactance]
    E --> F[Ideal Transformer Core]
    F --> G[X2 Secondary Leakage Reactance]
    G --> H[R2 Secondary Winding Resistance]
    H --> I[V2 Secondary Terminal]
    F --- F2[(Ideal Coupled Winding with turns ratio K)]
    F2 --- J[Secondary Path]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The magnetizing branch ($R_c$ parallel with $X_m$) is **shunt** (across the EMF), and the series impedances ($R_1, X_1, R_2', X_2'$) are **series** with the windings. After OC and SC tests, the entire primary-side leakage and secondary-side leakage are merged into single referred values $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$.

## 4.4 Mermaid: Functional Block — Lab Setup Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lab Workbench] --> B[Variac 0-270V 10A]
    A --> C[Transformer Under Test 2kVA]
    A --> D[Digital Meters V, A, W]
    A --> E[Connecting Wires and Shorting Bar]

    B --> C
    C --> D
    E --> C

    F[Pre-Test Checklist] --> G{Ratings Verified}
    G -->|OK| H[Connect LV for OC Test]
    G -->|Not OK| X[STOP - Check Nameplate]

    H --> I[Apply Rated Voltage Slowly]
    I --> J[Record V0 I0 W0]
    J --> K[Reduce Voltage to Zero]
    K --> L[Reconnect for SC Test - HV Side Energized]
    L --> M[Short LV Side]
    M --> N[Apply Small Voltage to Get Rated Current]
    N --> O[Record Vsc Isc Wsc]
    O --> P[Reduce Voltage and Switch Off]
    P --> Q[Tabulate and Calculate Parameters]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the OC test on a single-phase transformer conducted on the LV side and the SC test on the HV side?**

**Model Answer:**

The OC test is performed on the **LV side** because:
1. The rated voltage on the LV side is low (e.g., 230 V), making it convenient and safe to apply using a standard variac and meters.
2. The instruments used (voltmeter, ammeter, wattmeter) are typically rated for low-voltage applications, matching LV-side parameters.
3. Applying rated voltage on the HV side (e.g., 11 kV) would require specialized, expensive high-voltage meters.

The SC test is performed on the **HV side** because:
1. The SC test draws rated full-load current. On the HV side, this current is much smaller (since $I = S/V$), allowing the use of standard low-range ammeters.
2. The applied voltage is only 5-10% of rated; on the HV side, this is a manageable small voltage.
3. The LV side, which would carry a much larger short-circuit current, is kept shorted through a heavy copper bar that can safely handle the high current.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What information is obtained from the OC and SC tests on a single-phase transformer?**

**Model Answer:**

From the **OC test** (readings $V_0$, $I_0$, $W_0$):
- Core (iron) loss $P_i = W_0$
- No-load power factor $\cos \phi_0 = W_0 / (V_0 I_0)$
- Working and magnetizing components of no-load current
- Core-loss resistance $R_0$ and magnetizing reactance $X_0$

From the **SC test** (readings $V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $W_{sc}$):
- Full-load copper loss $P_{cu,FL} = W_{sc}$
- Equivalent impedance $Z_{01} = V_{sc} / I_{sc}$
- Equivalent resistance $R_{01} = W_{sc} / I_{sc}^2$
- Equivalent leakage reactance $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$

Together, these tests provide the complete equivalent circuit parameters needed to compute **efficiency** and **voltage regulation** at any load and power factor.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts. Internal choice is **between two main questions** (Q1 OR Q2, etc.), NOT within sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to conduct the OC and SC tests on a single-phase transformer. Mention the ratings and positions of the instruments used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 5 kVA, 200 V / 400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test results:
- OC Test (LV side): $V_0 = 200\ \text{V}$, $I_0 = 1.5\ \text{A}$, $W_0 = 75\ \text{W}$
- SC Test (HV side): $V_{sc} = 18\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 12.5\ \text{A}$, $W_{sc} = 100\ \text{W}$

Calculate: (i) Iron loss and full-load copper loss, (ii) Equivalent resistance and reactance referred to the HV side, (iii) Efficiency at 75% of full load and 0.85 p.f. lagging.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Circuit Diagram (described):**

The OC test circuit on the LV side consists of:
- A variac (autotransformer) connected between AC mains and the LV terminals of the transformer.
- A voltmeter (0-250 V) connected across the LV terminals.
- An ammeter (0-5 A) connected in series with the LV winding.
- A wattmeter with its current coil in series and pressure coil across the LV terminals.
- The HV terminals are left open.

**Procedure for OC Test:**
1. Ensure all connections are tight and HV side is open.
2. Set the variac to zero output.
3. Switch on the AC supply.
4. Slowly increase the variac output until the voltmeter reads the **rated LV voltage** (200 V in this case).
5. Note the readings of voltmeter, ammeter, and wattmeter.
6. Reduce variac to zero and switch off the supply.

**Procedure for SC Test:**
1. Reconnect instruments to the HV side.
2. Short-circuit the LV side using a thick copper bar of adequate current capacity.
3. Place the ammeter either in the LV short (to read large current) or HV side (to read smaller current). For our values, $I_{FL,LV} = 5000/200 = 25\ \text{A}$ and $I_{FL,HV} = 5000/400 = 12.5\ \text{A}$, so HV-side ammeter suffices.
4. Connect the variac to the HV side.
5. Gradually increase the variac output until the ammeter reads **rated HV current** (12.5 A).
6. Note all three meter readings. The voltmeter will read a small value (5-10% of rated).
7. Reduce voltage to zero and switch off.

**Ratings of Instruments:**
- **Voltmeter:** 0-250 V (for OC); 0-50 V (for SC)
- **Ammeter:** 0-5 A (for OC); 0-15 A (for SC)
- **Wattmeter:** 300 V, 5 A / 15 A dual range

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Iron loss and full-load copper loss:**

$$P_i = W_0 = 75\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{cu,FL} = W_{sc} = 100\ \text{W}$$

**(ii) Equivalent resistance and reactance referred to HV side:**

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{18}{12.5} = 1.44\ \Omega$$

$$R_{01} = \frac{W_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{100}{12.5^2} = \frac{100}{156.25} = 0.64\ \Omega$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2} = \sqrt{1.44^2 - 0.64^2} = \sqrt{2.0736 - 0.4096} = \sqrt{1.664} = 1.29\ \Omega$$

**(iii) Efficiency at 75% load, 0.85 p.f. lagging:**

Load fraction $x = 0.75$. Output power = $x \cdot S \cdot \cos \phi = 0.75 \times 5000 \times 0.85 = 3187.5\ \text{W}$.

Iron loss = 75 W. Copper loss at 75% load = $x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL} = 0.75^2 \times 100 = 0.5625 \times 100 = 56.25\ \text{W}$.

$$\eta = \frac{3187.5}{3187.5 + 75 + 56.25} = \frac{3187.5}{3318.75} = 0.9605 = 96.05\%$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the condition for **maximum efficiency** of a single-phase transformer. At what fraction of full load does maximum efficiency occur?

**(b) [7 Marks]** The OC and SC test data of a 10 kVA, 500 V / 250 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer are:
- OC Test (HV side): $V_0 = 500\ \text{V}$, $I_0 = 1.0\ \text{A}$, $W_0 = 100\ \text{W}$
- SC Test (LV side): $V_{sc} = 12\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 40\ \text{A}$, $W_{sc} = 200\ \text{W}$

Find the equivalent circuit parameters referred to the HV side, the voltage regulation at full load 0.8 p.f. lagging, and the load at which maximum efficiency occurs.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Derivation of Maximum Efficiency Condition:**

The efficiency at load fraction $x$ and p.f. $\cos \phi$ is:

$$\eta = \frac{x \, S \, \cos \phi}{x \, S \, \cos \phi + P_i + x^2 \, P_{cu,FL}}$$

For maximum efficiency, differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero. Equivalently, minimize the denominator for a given output. Since the numerator is fixed at constant output, we minimize:

$$f(x) = x \, S \, \cos \phi + P_i + x^2 \, P_{cu,FL}$$

$$\frac{df}{dx} = S \cos \phi + 2x \, P_{cu,FL} = 0$$

$$x = -\frac{S \cos \phi}{2 P_{cu,FL}}$$

This is a minimum only when the second derivative is positive, which it is ($\frac{d^2 f}{dx^2} = 2 P_{cu,FL} > 0$). However, $x$ should be positive — at maximum efficiency:

$$x_{max} = \frac{S \cos \phi}{2 P_{cu,FL}}$$

But more practically, we use the **load fraction** at which iron loss equals copper loss:

$$P_i = x^2 P_{cu,FL}$$

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}}$$

**Proof:** At $x_{max}$, $\eta$ is maximum when $P_i = x^2 P_{cu,FL}$, which means constant losses (iron) equal variable losses (copper). This is the classical transformer design principle — for distribution transformers designed to operate most of the time near full load, $P_i$ and $P_{cu,FL}$ are made equal so that the typical operating point gives maximum efficiency.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** 10 kVA, 500 V / 250 V, $K = 500/250 = 2$.

SC test was on the LV side with $I_{sc} = 40\ \text{A}$. Rated LV current = $10000/250 = 40\ \text{A}$ ✓.

**Equivalent impedance referred to LV side (where SC was done):**

$$Z_{01,L} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{12}{40} = 0.3\ \Omega$$

$$R_{01,L} = \frac{W_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{200}{40^2} = \frac{200}{1600} = 0.125\ \Omega$$

$$X_{01,L} = \sqrt{0.3^2 - 0.125^2} = \sqrt{0.09 - 0.015625} = \sqrt{0.074375} = 0.2727\ \Omega$$

**Refer to HV side by multiplying by $K^2 = 4$:**

$$R_{01,H} = 0.125 \times 4 = 0.5\ \Omega$$

$$X_{01,H} = 0.2727 \times 4 = 1.0908\ \Omega$$

**Voltage regulation at full load, 0.8 p.f. lagging (HV side):**

$I_{FL,H} = 10000/500 = 20\ \text{A}$, $V_2 = 500\ \text{V}$, $\cos \phi = 0.8$, $\sin \phi = 0.6$.

$$\%\text{Reg} = \frac{20 \times (0.5 \times 0.8 + 1.0908 \times 0.6)}{500} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{20 \times (0.4 + 0.6545)}{500} \times 100 = \frac{20 \times 1.0545}{500} \times 100 = \frac{21.09}{500} \times 100 = 4.218\%$$

**Load at maximum efficiency:**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{200}} = \sqrt{0.5} = 0.7071 = 70.71\%$$

**Result Summary:**
- $R_{01,H} = 0.5\ \Omega$, $X_{01,H} = 1.09\ \Omega$
- Voltage regulation = **4.22%**
- Maximum efficiency occurs at **70.71% of full load**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to refer parameters:** A common mistake is computing regulation using HV-side values while the load voltage is on the LV side, or vice versa. **Always refer all parameters to the same side** as the load terminal voltage used.
> 2. **Sign error in regulation formula:** For leading p.f., the $X \sin \phi$ term has a **minus** sign. A plus sign will give a wrong (always-positive) regulation that loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $V_0$ and $V_{sc}$:** In OC, $V_0$ is **rated** voltage. In SC, $V_{sc}$ is only 5-10% of rated. Mixing them up gives garbage values.
> 4. **Square root domain error:** If you compute $Z^2 < R^2$, you have a sign or measurement error. $X_{01}$ cannot be imaginary.
> 5. **Iron loss ≠ copper loss by default:** Many students write $P_i = P_{cu,FL}$ as if they are always equal. They are equal **only at the maximum-efficiency load point**, not in general.
> 6. **Power factor at no-load is low:** Students often assume $\cos \phi_0 = 0.8$ like a typical load. The actual no-load p.f. is around 0.2-0.3. Forgetting this changes the value of $I_w$ and $I_\mu$ drastically.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: OC & SC Test**

- **OC Test purpose:** Determines **iron loss $P_i$**, **no-load current $I_0$**, and **magnetizing branch parameters** ($R_0$, $X_0$).
- **SC Test purpose:** Determines **full-load copper loss $P_{cu,FL}$** and **equivalent series parameters** ($R_{01}$, $X_{01}$).
- **OC side:** LV side energized, HV side open.
- **SC side:** HV side energized, LV side shorted.
- **Wattmeter reading interpretation:** $W_0 = P_i$ (iron loss), $W_{sc} = P_{cu,FL}$ (full-load copper loss).
- **No-load power factor:** Very low, $\cos \phi_0 = W_0 / (V_0 I_0) \approx 0.2 - 0.3$.
- **Equivalent resistance:** $R_{01} = W_{sc} / I_{sc}^2$ (referred to test side).
- **Equivalent reactance:** $X_{01} = \sqrt{(V_{sc}/I_{sc})^2 - R_{01}^2}$.
- **Refer to other side:** Divide by $K^2$ (or multiply by $K^2$).
- **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = (x S \cos \phi) / (x S \cos \phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,FL})$.
- **Voltage regulation (lagging):** $\%\text{Reg} = [I_2 (R_{02} \cos \phi + X_{02} \sin \phi) / V_2] \times 100$.
- **Voltage regulation (leading):** Sign of $X_{02} \sin \phi$ is **negative**.
- **Maximum efficiency condition:** $P_i = x^2 P_{cu,FL}$, so $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,FL}}$.
- **Why two tests, not one?** Iron losses depend on $V^2$, copper losses on $I^2$. OC is full-V low-I; SC is low-V full-I. Orthogonal conditions isolate each loss.
- **Apparent power identity:** $S = V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$ (ideal).
- **KTU lab safety:** Always start variac from zero, ensure cooling, never leave SC test running for more than a few minutes (the windings heat up rapidly under short-circuit conditions since the cooling is designed for full-voltage full-load operation, not full-current low-voltage continuous duty).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study the characteristics of P-I controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL607 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND MECHATRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study the characteristics of P-I controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **P-I (Proportional-Integral) Controller** is a linear, continuous-time feedback controller that combines two control actions — **Proportional (P)** action and **Integral (I)** action — to drive the process variable (PV) toward the desired setpoint (SP). In the time domain, the controller output is mathematically expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = controller output signal (manipulated variable, MV)
- $e(t) = SP - PV$ = error signal
- $K_p$ = proportional gain (dimensionless or in %/%)
- $K_i$ = integral gain (sec⁻¹)

In the **frequency (s) domain**, the transfer function of a non-interacting (ideal) PI controller is:

$$G_{PI}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = K_p\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i\, s}\right)$$

where $T_i = K_p / K_i$ is the **integral time** (or *reset time*), measured in seconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim expected):** A P-I controller is a two-term controller whose output is the linear sum of a term proportional to the instantaneous error and a term proportional to the integral of the error over time. It is widely used in process industries because it eliminates the steady-state offset produced by a pure P controller while retaining adequate speed of response.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cruise Control of Your Mind" 🚗

Imagine you are driving a car and trying to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a flat highway. Your **eyes** measure the speedometer reading, your **brain** compares it with 60, and your **foot** presses the accelerator.

| Driver's Action | Controller Equivalent | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| You press the accelerator **harder** the more you are *behind* the target speed | **Proportional (P) action** | Reacts to *present* error; bigger error → bigger push |
| You keep a small, steady extra pressure for as long as **any** error remains | **Integral (I) action** | Reacts to *accumulated past* error; eliminates the leftover gap |
| Pure P-only driver always leaves a small speed gap (say 58 km/h instead of 60) | **Steady-state offset** | Because P cannot fully overcome constant disturbances |
| P + I driver finally settles at exactly 60 km/h | **Zero steady-state error** | The integral keeps nudging until error = 0 |

So **P** is your *reflex* and **I** is your *memory* — together they give you both **speed** and **accuracy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study the characteristics?** In a B.Tech lab (PCMUL607), this experiment trains the student to:
> 1. Wire / configure a PI controller module on a process-control trainer.
> 2. Apply a step setpoint change and observe the response of a simulated plant.
> 3. Plot the **time response** and infer the effect of $K_p$ and $K_i$ on **rise time, peak overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error**.
> 4. Compare the P, I, and PI responses to appreciate the **offset elimination** property of integral action.

---

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (Must be Memorised)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proportional Band | $PB$ | **%** | $PB = (100 / K_p) \times 100$ — range of error over which output swings full scale |
| Proportional Gain | $K_p$ | unitless | Controller sensitivity to current error |
| Integral Time (Reset Time) | $T_i$ | **seconds** | Time required for the I-action to repeat the P-action contribution |
| Integral Gain | $K_i$ | sec⁻¹ | Strength of the memory action |
| Derivative Time (for reference) | $T_d$ | **seconds** | Not used in PI; only in PID |
| Steady-state Error | $e_{ss}$ | same as PV | Residual error after transients die out |

> [!TIP]
> **Proportional Band trick for exams:** If $K_p = 4$, then $PB = 25\,\%$. If $PB = 50\,\%$, then $K_p = 2$. Examiners love asking this conversion — practice it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a PI Controller (Why it works the way it does)

A PI controller has two **parallel branches** summed at the output:

1. **P-branch:** Multiplies the present error $e(t)$ by $K_p$. Acts **instantaneously**.
2. **I-branch:** Integrates the error over time and multiplies by $K_i$. Acts on the **history** of the error.

### Why combine them?
- Pure P → fast but always leaves an **offset** (because a non-zero error is needed to keep the actuator working against a constant load).
- Pure I → eventually accurate but extremely **slow** and may oscillate.
- PI → **fast** (from P) and **accurate** (from I). The I-branch keeps integrating the residual error until it forces the steady-state error to **exactly zero**.

---

## 2.2 Operational Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Measure** the process variable $PV(t)$ using a sensor.
2. **Compute** the error: $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$.
3. **P-action** instantly scales the error: $u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$.
4. **I-action** accumulates the error: $u_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau$.
5. **Sum** the two: $u(t) = u_P(t) + u_I(t)$.
6. **Apply** $u(t)$ to the final control element (valve, motor, heater).
7. **Repeat** at the next sampling instant (continuous-time version updates continuously).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt$ | Time-domain PI output | same as MV | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $G_{PI}(s) = K_p + K_i/s$ | Laplace-domain transfer function | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $G_{PI}(s) = K_p(1 + 1/(T_i s))$ | Standard form | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $T_i = K_p / K_i$ | Relation between $T_i$ and $K_i$ | seconds | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $PB = 100 / K_p$ | Proportional Band | % | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $e_{ss}$ (P-only, step input) $= R / (1 + K_p K_{process})$ | Steady-state offset for P controller | same as SP | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $e_{ss}$ (PI, step input) $= 0$ | PI eliminates step-input offset | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Closed-loop TF (1st-order plant $K/(\tau s + 1)$ with PI) | $T(s) = \dfrac{K K_p (T_i s + 1)}{T_i s (\tau s + 1) + K K_p (T_i s + 1)}$ | — | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Type of system: PI on Type-0 plant → **Type 1** | One integrator in open loop | — | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Bode: PI adds +20 dB/dec slope below $1/T_i$ | Frequency-response shape | dB, rad/s | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions / sanity checks**
> - If $K_i = 0$ → controller reduces to pure P.
> - If $K_p = 0$ → controller reduces to pure I (very slow, often unstable when used alone).
> - For **anti-windup** in real hardware, the integrator is clamped when the actuator saturates — this is the **clamping / back-calculation** method, often asked in viva.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of PI Controllers

- **Thermal furnaces / ovens** (temperature control) — by far the most common industrial application.
- **Motor speed control** in small DC drives.
- **Level control** in tanks (using level transmitter + control valve).
- **Flow control** in pipelines (cascade PI loops).
- **HVAC systems** (chilled-water valves, air-handling units).
- **Robotics & mechatronics** — inner position/speed loops.

> About **90 – 95 % of all industrial control loops** in the world use a PI or PID controller. The PI is preferred over PID when the measurement is **noisy** (because derivative action amplifies noise). This is a very common viva question: *"Why PI and not PID?"* — answer: derivative amplifies measurement noise and is hard to tune; PI suffices for most first-order-lag-dominated processes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the PI Controller Output Equation

We start from the basic definition that a linear combination of error and its integral is the controller output.

**Step 1 — Proportional action alone**

$$u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$$

This says: *the further we are from the target, the harder we push.*

**Step 2 — Integral action alone**

The integral of the error represents the **accumulated past history**. A small persistent error, when integrated over a long time, can grow large and force the system to correct.

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Combine (superposition principle for linear systems)**

$$u(t) = u_P(t) + u_I(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Laplace domain for analysis**

Using $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau\} = \dfrac{E(s)}{s}$:

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} \, E(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) E(s)$$

**Step 5 — Transfer function of the PI controller**

$$G_{PI}(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$$

**Step 6 — Standard "textbook" form**

Factor out $K_p$:

$$G_{PI}(s) = K_p\!\left(1 + \frac{K_i}{K_p\, s}\right) = K_p\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i\, s}\right), \quad \text{where} \quad T_i = \frac{K_p}{K_i}$$

This is the form used in most process-control textbooks and in the KTU lab manuals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PI Eliminates Steady-State Error (the most important KTU derivation)

Consider a **first-order process** with transfer function:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

and a **PI controller** $G_c(s) = K_p\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right)$.

**Open-loop transfer function:**

$$G(s) = G_c(s) \, G_p(s) = K_p\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right) \cdot \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

$$G(s) = \frac{K\, K_p \,(T_i s + 1)}{T_i s \,(\tau s + 1)}$$

**Count the integrators (Type of system):** The denominator contains **one** factor of $s$ in the origin → this is a **Type 1 system**.

**Steady-state error for a unit step input** $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

Substitute $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

Now compute $G(0)$:

$$G(0) = \frac{K K_p (0 + 1)}{T_i \cdot 0 \cdot (1)} \to \frac{\text{finite}}{\text{zero}} \to \infty$$

Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Conclusion for the answer script:** A PI controller (containing one integrator) placed in the forward path of a Type-0 plant makes the closed-loop system **Type 1**, which guarantees **zero steady-state error for a step setpoint change** — the fundamental reason the I term is added to the P term.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Why Pure-P Has Offset (for comparison — a KTU favourite)

Take the same plant $G_p(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$ with a **pure P controller** $G_c(s) = K_p$.

**Open-loop:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K K_p}{\tau s + 1}$ → no integrator → **Type 0**.

For unit step input:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p K}$$

This is **non-zero** for any finite $K_p$. For example, if $K = 2$ and $K_p = 1$, then $e_{ss} = 1/3$. This is the **offset** that the I term removes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Effect of Increasing $K_p$ (qualitative, expected in viva)

A useful analytical expression: for a P-only closed loop, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{K K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K K_p}$$

Comparing with a first-order form $T(s) = \dfrac{K_{cl}}{\tau_{cl} s + 1}$:

$$K_{cl} = \frac{K K_p}{1 + K K_p}, \qquad \tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau}{1 + K K_p}$$

Observations:
- As $K_p \uparrow$, $K_{cl} \to 1$ (good — the output follows the setpoint).
- As $K_p \uparrow$, $\tau_{cl} \downarrow$ (faster response).
- But $e_{ss} = 1 - K_{cl} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K K_p}$ → still non-zero.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Step Response Characteristics for PI (qualitative)

For a PI-controlled first-order plant, the response is typically **second-order** because the closed-loop denominator becomes quadratic. The characteristic equation:

$$T_i s (\tau s + 1) + K K_p (T_i s + 1) = 0$$

$$\tau T_i s^2 + T_i s + K K_p T_i s + K K_p = 0$$

$$\tau T_i s^2 + T_i(1 + K K_p) s + K K_p = 0$$

Divide by $\tau T_i$:

$$s^2 + \frac{(1 + K K_p)}{\tau} s + \frac{K K_p}{\tau T_i} = 0$$

Compare with $s^2 + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K K_p}{\tau T_i}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{1 + K K_p}{2 \sqrt{K K_p \, \tau \, T_i}}$$

Effects (memorise for the lab report and viva):

| Action | Increases $K_p$ | Increases $K_i$ (decreases $T_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| Rise time | **decreases** | slight decrease |
| Peak overshoot | **increases** | **increases** |
| Settling time | roughly unchanged at first, then increases | **increases sharply** |
| Steady-state error | decreases (but not to zero) | goes to **exactly zero** |
| Stability margin | decreases — may oscillate | decreases — integral wind-up risk |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram (PI Controller in a Process Control Loop)

This is the diagram the KTU examiner expects in your lab record for **any** controller experiment. Note the **negative feedback** convention.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SETPOINT R(s)"] --> B["COMPARATOR\n(+/-)"]
    B --> C["PI CONTROLLER\nGc(s) = Kp + Ki/s"]
    C --> D["ACTUATOR\n(Valve / Motor / Heater)"]
    D --> E["PROCESS PLANT\nGp(s) = K/(taus+1)"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT PV(s)"]
    F --> G["SENSOR\nH(s)"]
    G --> B
    F --> H["ERROR SIGNAL\ne(t) = SP - PV"]
    classDef ctrlStyle fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef procStyle fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a
    class C ctrlStyle
    class E procStyle
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The output $PV(s)$ is subtracted from the setpoint $R(s)$ to form the error $E(s)$, which is fed to the PI controller. The controller output drives the actuator, which manipulates the plant, producing a new $PV(s)$. The loop runs continuously.

---

## 4.2 Internal Structure of the PI Controller Itself

The PI controller is a **parallel combination** of a P-block and an I-block. The two outputs are summed before being sent to the actuator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Error e(t)"] --> P["P BLOCK\nGain = Kp"]
    IN --> I["I BLOCK\nIntegrator 1/s\nGain = Ki"]
    P --> SUMMER["SUMMER\n(+)"]
    I --> SUMMER
    SUMMER --> OUT["Controller Output\nu(t) = Kp e + Ki INTe"]
    classDef intStyle fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef sumStyle fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a
    class I intStyle
    class SUMMER sumStyle
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The same error $e(t)$ is fed in **parallel** to two blocks. The upper (P) path multiplies $e(t)$ by $K_p$ instantly. The lower (I) path integrates $e(t)$ and scales by $K_i$. The two contributions are added in the summer to form the final control signal $u(t)$.

---

## 4.3 Typical Experimental Trainer Setup (KTU Lab)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PC["Personal Computer\nwith LabVIEW / MATLAB"]
    DAQ["Data Acquisition Card\n(PCI / USB)"]
    TRAINER["Process Control Trainer Kit\n(P, I, PI, PD, PID modules)"]
    PLANT["Analog Process Simulator\n(thermal / level / pressure)"]
    SCOPE["Digital Storage Oscilloscope\n(alternative to PC plot)"]
    FG["Function Generator\n(step / square wave input)"]
    FG --> TRAINER
    TRAINER --> PLANT
    PLANT --> TRAINER
    TRAINER --> DAQ
    DAQ --> PC
    PLANT --> SCOPE
    classDef hwStyle fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#c9184a,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef pcStyle fill:#cdb4db,stroke:#7b2cbf,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a
    class TRAINER,PLANT,SCOPE,FG hwStyle
    class PC,DAQ pcStyle
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A function generator injects a step (or square-wave) disturbance/reference. The trainer's PI module acts on the error between reference and the plant's measured output. The plant simulator (thermal tank, level tank, etc.) responds. The closed-loop signals are captured by a DAQ card into a PC for plotting, or directly viewed on a DSO.

---

## 4.4 Expected Step Response Curves (Schematic for Lab Notebook)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P_ONLY["P-Only Response"]
        P1["Overshoots target"] --> P2["Settles below setpoint"]
        P2 --> P3["Steady-state OFFSET remains"]
    end
    subgraph PI_RESP["PI Response"]
        I1["Small or no overshoot"] --> I2["Brief oscillation"] --> I3["Settles EXACTLY at setpoint"]
    end
    P_ONLY ~~~ PI_RESP
    classDef badStyle fill:#ffadad,stroke:#c9184a,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef goodStyle fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#1a1a1a
    class P1,P2,P3 badStyle
    class I1,I2,I3 goodStyle
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This side-by-side conceptual plot explains **why the I term is added**. The P-only curve plateaus *below* the setpoint (offset). The PI curve oscillates slightly and then lands *exactly* on the setpoint. This single picture is worth 3–4 marks in the lab record.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme pattern** for lab courses (PCMUL607). Two short Part-A questions (3 marks each) and two Part-B long-answer questions (14 marks each, internal choice) are provided. Marks are split 7 + 7 within each Part-B.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCMUL607]** 
*Define a PI controller. Write its mathematical model in time and frequency domain, and explain the role of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A PI controller is a two-term linear feedback controller whose output is the sum of a term proportional to the instantaneous error and a term proportional to the integral of the error.

Time-domain form: $\;u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \displaystyle\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$  …… (1 mark)

Frequency-domain form: $\;G_{PI}(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} = K_p\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right)$ …… (1 mark)

**Role:** The **P-term** ($K_p e$) provides an **instantaneous** corrective action proportional to the *current* error — gives **speed**. The **I-term** ($K_i \int e\,dt$) accumulates **past** errors and drives the steady-state error to **zero** — gives **accuracy**. …… (1 mark)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCMUL607]**
*A pure P-controller drives a process with $G_p(s) = 2/(10s+1)$. With $K_p = 1$, calculate the steady-state error for a unit step input. If the same process is now controlled by a PI controller with the same $K_p$, what will the steady-state error be? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For P-only, open-loop $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{10s+1}$ is a **Type-0** system.

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p K} = \frac{1}{1 + 1 \times 2} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333 \quad ……(1\text{ mark})$$

With PI, $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s$ adds one integrator → open loop becomes **Type-1**.

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0}\frac{1}{1 + G(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0}\frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0 \quad ……(1\text{ mark})$$

**Justification:** The integral term in the PI controller forces the steady-state error to **exactly zero** for a step input, eliminating the offset inherent in pure-P control. ……(1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCMUL607, Module Exp. 2]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a closed-loop process-control system showing the PI controller in the forward path. Identify each block and the signal at every node. Explain the function of the comparator and the feedback sensor. … **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the transfer function of an ideal PI controller from its time-domain equation. For a first-order plant $G_p(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$ with PI controller, show mathematically that the steady-state error to a unit step input is **zero**. … **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks

**Block diagram (5 marks for the drawing, 2 marks for the explanation):**

The student must draw the standard unity-feedback block diagram:

```
   R(s)──►(+)──E(s)──►[Gc(s)=Kp+Ki/s]──U(s)──►[Gp(s)]──Y(s)──┐
           ▲(-)                                                    │
           │                                                       │
           └──────────────[H(s) = 1 (unity)]──────────────────────┘
```

- **Setpoint $R(s)$:** Desired value entered by the operator. ……(0.5 mark)
- **Comparator (summing junction with + and – signs):** Computes the **error** $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$. ……(0.5 mark)
- **PI Controller $G_c(s)$:** Generates the control signal $U(s) = K_p E(s) + (K_i/s) E(s)$. ……(0.5 mark)
- **Actuator (often included inside $G_p$ or shown separately):** Converts $U(s)$ to physical action (valve opening, heater power, etc.). ……(0.5 mark)
- **Process Plant $G_p(s)$:** The system being controlled — temperature, level, speed, etc. ……(0.5 mark)
- **Output $Y(s)$:** Measured process variable. ……(0.5 mark)
- **Feedback Sensor $H(s)$:** Converts the physical PV back to a comparable signal (e.g., RTD, LVDT, tachogenerator). In unity feedback $H(s) = 1$. ……(0.5 mark)
- **Negative sign at the comparator:** Implements **negative feedback**, the heart of closed-loop stability. ……(0.5 mark)
- *Function of comparator:* Produces an error signal that drives the controller. ……(0.5 mark)
- *Function of sensor:* Provides a measurement of the output so that the loop can self-correct. ……(0.5 mark)
- Loop direction: counter-clockwise from $R(s)$ through the plant back via $H(s)$ to the comparator. ……(0.5 mark)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Time-domain equation (1 mark):**

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Apply Laplace transform (1 mark):**

Using $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$ and $\mathcal{L}\!\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{E(s)}{s}$:

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Transfer function (1 mark):**

$$G_{PI}(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = K_p\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right), \quad T_i = \frac{K_p}{K_i}$$

**Step 4 — Open-loop TF with the given plant (1 mark):**

$$G(s) = G_{PI}(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{K K_p (T_i s + 1)}{T_i s (\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the system type (1 mark):** Denominator has **one** free factor of $s$ → **Type-1** system.

**Step 6 — Steady-state error for unit step (1 mark):**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and conclude (1 mark):**

As $s \to 0$, the numerator $K K_p (T_i s + 1) \to K K_p$ (finite) and the denominator $T_i s (\tau s + 1) \to 0$. Hence $G(s) \to \infty$, and:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 marks) — *Internal alternative*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCMUL607, Module Exp. 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **experimental setup** used in your lab to study the characteristics of a PI controller. List the apparatus, the procedure, and the observations to be recorded. … **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the step-response of a PI-controlled first-order plant, define and explain the meaning of **rise time, peak overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error**. Sketch the typical step response and indicate these quantities on it. Discuss how varying $K_p$ and $K_i$ affect each. … **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Apparatus (2 marks):**
- Process-control trainer kit with P, I, PI, PD, PID plug-in modules.
- Analog process simulator (thermal / level / pressure unit).
- Function generator (for step / square-wave input).
- PC with DAQ card + LabVIEW / MATLAB (or) Digital Storage Oscilloscope.
- Connecting leads and patch cords.

**Block diagram of the setup (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.3** of these notes and re-draw it cleanly with a ruler in the answer script.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Connect the trainer, process simulator, and PC/DSO as per the block diagram.
2. Insert the PI module into the controller slot; set $K_p$ to a moderate value and $K_i = 0$ initially (i.e., start in P-only mode).
3. Apply a **step setpoint change** (say from 0 V to a calibrated value, e.g., 5 V) using the function generator.
4. Record the **process variable** vs. time response on the PC/DSO.
5. Tabulate the values: time, $PV(t)$, $u(t)$ (controller output), and $e(t)$.
6. Repeat for **three different values of $K_p$** (low, medium, high), keeping $K_i = 0$ (pure P case).
7. Now set $K_i$ to a small value and re-run the experiment; then increase $K_i$ in steps and record responses.
8. Plot **PV(t) vs. t** for each case on a single graph for comparison.
9. From the plots, compute rise time, peak overshoot, settling time, and $e_{ss}$ for each case.

**Observations to be tabulated (1 mark):** A table with columns: $K_p$, $K_i$, $t_r$, $M_p$ (overshoot %), $t_s$, $e_{ss}$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**Definitions (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Rise time $t_r$:** Time taken for the response to rise from **10 % to 90 %** of its final value (or 0 % to 100 %, depending on convention). Indicates **speed**.
2. **Peak overshoot $M_p$:** The amount by which the response **exceeds** the final steady value, expressed as a percentage. Indicates **stability margin**.
3. **Settling time $t_s$:** Time required for the response to stay within $\pm 2\,\%$ (or $\pm 5\,\%$) of the final value. Indicates how quickly transients die out.
4. **Steady-state error $e_{ss}$:** The **residual error** between setpoint and PV after all transients have settled. For PI on a step input, $e_{ss} = 0$.

**Sketch of typical PI step response (2 marks):** Draw a curve that rises, may overshoot slightly, oscillates, and finally settles **exactly** on the setpoint. Mark $t_r$, $M_p$, $t_s$, and the (zero) $e_{ss}$ on it.

**Effect of varying gains (2 marks):**

| Parameter increased | $t_r$ | $M_p$ | $t_s$ | $e_{ss}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ for large $K_p$ | ↓ (but not 0) |
| $K_i$ ↑ (i.e., $T_i$ ↓) | slight ↓ | ↑ sharply | ↑ sharply | → **0** |

> "↑" = increases, "↓" = decreases. Use these arrows precisely in your answer.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 6 ways KTU students LOSE marks on this experiment — avoid them!**
>
> 1. **Confusing $K_i$ and $T_i$.** They are reciprocally related ($T_i = K_p/K_i$). If the question gives $T_i$ in seconds, don't write $K_i = T_i$ — that costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to mark the negative sign** at the comparator in the block diagram. Examiners specifically look for **negative feedback**; positive feedback here means 0 marks for the diagram.
> 3. **Stating that "PI removes offset"** without a derivation. Always show that the open-loop system becomes **Type 1** and hence $e_{ss} = 0$ for a step. The reasoning matters.
> 4. **Mixing up P, I, and PI plots** when drawing the comparison. Remember: P has offset, I has no offset but slow/oscillatory, PI has no offset and is faster.
> 5. **Writing "Kp × e(t)" as "P-controller"** in a derivation question. The question asks for the *PI* output, not just the P-output — always write the full sum $K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt$.
> 6. **No units on the time-response axes.** The graph MUST have axes labelled: time in *seconds* on x-axis, PV in *volts* (or °C, cm, etc. depending on plant) on y-axis. A graph without labelled axes loses 1 mark in the lab record.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ PI controller output: $\;u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \displaystyle\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau$
- ✅ Transfer function: $\;G_{PI}(s) = K_p + K_i/s = K_p(1 + 1/(T_i s))$
- ✅ $T_i = K_p / K_i$ (integral / reset time, in **seconds**)
- ✅ Proportional Band: $\;PB = 100 / K_p$ (in **%**)
- ✅ P-only has **steady-state offset** $= R/(1+K_pK)$ for a unit step; **PI has zero offset**.
- ✅ PI makes a Type-0 plant behave as **Type 1** for step inputs.
- ✅ Increasing $K_p$ → faster but more overshoot and possible oscillation.
- ✅ Increasing $K_i$ → drives offset to zero but can cause **integral wind-up** (use anti-windup in practice).
- ✅ PI is preferred over PID for **noisy** measurements (no derivative amplification of noise).
- ✅ Block diagram: **setpoint → comparator (with –) → PI controller → actuator → plant → output → sensor → back to comparator**.
- ✅ Lab procedure: apply step input → record PV(t) → tabulate $t_r, M_p, t_s, e_{ss}$ → plot for different $K_p$ and $K_i$ → compare with P-only.
- ✅ Viva question: *"Why not just use a very high $K_p$?"* → Answer: high $K_p$ causes overshoot, oscillation, actuator saturation, and still leaves a small offset; I-action solves the offset problem.
- ✅ Viva question: *"What is integral wind-up?"* → When the actuator saturates, the integrator keeps accumulating error; the moment the setpoint is reached, the integrator is so charged that the output shoots past the limit. Fix: **clamping** or **back-calculation** anti-windup schemes.
- ✅ Viva question: *"Can a PI controller stabilise an unstable plant?"* → A PI controller alone cannot stabilise a plant that has poles in the right half-plane for **all** values of $K_p, K_i$. Pole-placement or state feedback is needed.
- ✅ Memorise: **PI ≈ 90 % of industrial loops**; **PID ≈ 9 %**; **others ≈ 1 %** (advanced / model-predictive).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study the characteristics of RTD and thermocouple
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUL607 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND MECHATRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study the characteristics of RTD and thermocouple # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)? An **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)** is a precision temperature sensor that exploits the predictable, linear variation of the electrical resistance of a pure metal with changes in temperature. As the temperature rises, the metallic lattice vibrates more vigorously, increasing electron scattering and hence electrical resistance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** "A Resistance Temperature Detector is a passive transducer whose resistance varies as a known, repeatable function of temperature, typically employing platinum as the sensing element due to its chemical stability, linearity, and wide operating range." The most common industrial RTD is the **PT100** — a Platinum sensor with a nominal resistance of exactly **100 Ω at 0 °C** and a mean temperature coefficient (alpha, α) of **0.00385 Ω/Ω/°C** (the European/IEC standard "385" curve). ### Conceptual Analogy: The "Stretchy Wire Ruler" Imagine a metal wire as a tightly packed row of marbles in a tube. When you heat the tube, the marbles (atoms) start jiggling and bumping into each other more often, making it harder for electricity (the small ball you push through) to pass. The wire "resists" more. Now, you can **calibrate** the wire: at 0 °C, it allows exactly 100 units through; at 100 °C, it allows about 138.5 units. So, by simply *measuring resistance*, you can read the temperature — just like reading a ruler by looking at how far a metal strip has expanded, but here you measure the *electrical* expansion. --- ## 1.2 What is a Thermocouple? A **thermocouple** is a temperature sensor made by joining two *dissimilar* metals at one end (the **hot / measuring junction**) while keeping the other ends open at a known reference temperature (the **cold / reference junction**). The temperature difference between the two junctions generates a small DC voltage called the **Seebeck EMF**, named after Thomas Johann Seebeck who discovered it in 1821. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** "A thermocouple is an active transducer based on the Seebeck Effect; it converts a temperature gradient between two dissimilar-metal junctions directly into a measurable electromotive force (EMF), which is a non-linear function of the temperature difference." Common thermocouple types standardized by ANSI/ISA letter codes include: - **Type K** (Chromel–Alumel) → range −200 °C to +1260 °C (most popular) - **Type J** (Iron–Constantan) → range 0 °C to +760 °C - **Type T** (Copper–Constantan) → range −200 °C to +370 °C - **Type R / S** (Platinum–Rhodium) → high-temperature lab use ### Conceptual Analogy: The "Two Friends Walking Together" Picture two friends, A and B, walking together on a hot beach. Friend A is wearing dark cotton (heats up fast), and Friend B is wearing white linen (heats up slowly). Connect them with a small wire (the thermocouple). Because their electrons heat up at different rates, a tiny "disagreement voltage" appears between them — the warmer end pushes electrons harder. The bigger the temperature difference between the hot junction (on the sand) and the cold junction (in the shade), the bigger this tiny voltage. That disagreement *is* the Seebeck EMF, and it tells you exactly how hot the sand is once you know the shade temperature. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights) | Parameter | Standard Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | PT100 Resistance at 0 °C | **100 Ω** | IEC 60751 | | PT1000 Resistance at 0 °C | **1000 Ω** | Higher sensitivity | | Platinum alpha (α) | **0.00385 /°C** | IEC 385 curve | | Nickel alpha (α) | **0.00618 /°C** | Less stable than Pt | | Seebeck coefficient (Type K) | **≈ 41 µV/°C** at 20 °C | Approx. linear in mid-range | | Standard cold-junction temperature | **0 °C (ice bath)** | Reference standard | | Typical thermocouple output | **mV range** (few to ~50 mV) | Requires amplification | --- ## 1.4 Why Study Both? — KTU Syllabus Context > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PCMUL607 – Module 2):** Students must *plot* and *compare* the static characteristics (output vs. temperature) of an RTD and a thermocouple on the same graph. This builds intuition on linearity, sensitivity, range, and transducer classification (passive vs. active, Class A vs. Class B, etc.) — a frequently repeated 14-mark question in KTU ESE. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 RTD — Working Principle and Math A pure metal's resistance rises with temperature because increased lattice vibrations (phonons) scatter conduction electrons more frequently. For small ranges, the relationship is **linear**: $$R_T \;=\; R_0 \bigl(1 + \alpha T\bigr)$$ where: - $R_T$ = resistance at temperature $T$ (in °C) - $R_0$ = resistance at the reference temperature (0 °C) — for PT100, $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ - $\alpha$ = mean temperature coefficient of resistance (per °C) - $T$ = temperature in °C For higher accuracy over a wider range (e.g., −200 °C to +850 °C for platinum), the **Callendar-Van Dusen equation** is used: $$R_T \;=\; R_0 \bigl[\,1 + A T + B T^2 + C\,(T-100)\,T^3\,\bigr] \quad \text{for } T < 0\,^\circ\text{C}$$ $$R_T \;=\; R_0 \bigl[\,1 + A T + B T^2\,\bigr] \quad \text{for } 0 \le T \le 850\,^\circ\text{C}$$ For PT100 (IEC 60751): - $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{°C}^{-1}$ - $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{°C}^{-2}$ - $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{°C}^{-4}$ ### The "Why" Behind the Steps - **Step 1 — Energy balance:** Heating increases phonon density → increased electron–phonon scattering → resistance rises. - **Step 2 — Linearization:** In a small T-window, the parabolic term $B T^2$ is negligible, so we keep only the first-order term. - **Step 3 — Lead compensation (3-wire / 4-wire Kelvin):** RTD wires themselves have resistance; without compensation, long lead lengths cause errors. A 3-wire bridge or 4-wire Kelvin connection cancels the lead resistance. --- ## 2.2 Thermocouple — Working Principle and Math A thermocouple is governed by the **Seebeck Effect** and three supporting laws: ### (a) Law of Homogeneous Circuits > *"An EMF cannot be generated in a thermocouple circuit by simply applying temperature to a single homogeneous conductor; the EMF depends only on the two dissimilar metals and the temperature difference between their junctions."* ### (b) Law of Intermediate Metals > *"Inserting a third metal into the circuit does not change the EMF, provided its two new junctions are at the same temperature."* — This is why you can solder/connect extension wires and instrumentation leads safely. ### (c) Law of Intermediate Temperatures > *"The EMF generated with junctions at T1 and T3 equals the algebraic sum of EMFs at (T1, T2) and (T2, T3)."* — This is the foundation of **cold-junction compensation (CJC)** in modern instruments. ### Mathematical Form The Seebeck EMF is the integral of the difference of the two metals' Seebeck coefficients over the temperature gradient: $$E_{AB} \;=\; \int_{T_\text{ref}}^{T_\text{hot}} \bigl[\,S_A(T) - S_B(T)\,\bigr]\, dT$$ In practice, manufacturers publish **polynomial reference tables** (ITS-90). For Type K, a simplified 8th-order fit is: $$E(T) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{n} c_i\, T^{i}$$ with the leading coefficients (NIST ITS-90, T in °C, E in µV, valid −270 to 1372 °C): - $c_0 = -1.76004 \times 10^{1}$ - $c_1 = +3.89212 \times 10^{1}$ - $c_2 = +1.85588 \times 10^{-2}$ - … (omitted higher orders for brevity) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Because $E$ is a function of *temperature difference*, the thermocouple *always* needs a known reference (cold) junction. Modern instruments "simulate" a 0 °C reference electronically — this is **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)**. Cold-junction compensation is a **direct application of the Law of Intermediate Temperatures**. --- ## 2.3 Static Characteristics — Side-by-Side | Property | RTD (PT100) | Thermocouple (Type K) | |---|---|---| | Principle | Resistance change with T | Seebeck EMF | | Transducer class | **Passive** (needs excitation) | **Active** (self-generating) | | Output quantity | Resistance (Ω) | Voltage (mV) | | Output range | ~80 to ~400 Ω | ~−6 to +50 mV | | Sensitivity | **~0.385 Ω/°C** (PT100) | **~41 µV/°C** | | Linearity | **Excellent** (near-linear) | **Poor to moderate** (polynomial) | | Accuracy | **±0.1 °C** (Class A) | **±1 °C to ±2.2 °C** (Standard) | | Range | −200 to +850 °C | −200 to +1260 °C | | Response time | **Slower** (thermal mass) | **Very fast** (tiny junction) | | Cost | Higher (Pt is expensive) | Lowest (robust, cheap) | | Long-term stability | Excellent | Drifts with age / corrosion | | Self-heating error | **Yes** (excitation I²R heating) | **No** (no excitation needed) | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **RTDs:** HVAC duct sensors, pharmaceutical reactors, calibration baths, precision lab thermometry, aerospace engine inlets. - **Thermocouples:** Industrial furnaces, gas-turbine exhausts, kiln profiling, food processing, plastic injection-mold hot-runner systems — wherever extreme heat, fast response, and ruggedness matter more than precision. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the exam:** **"RTD = Resistance / Repeats / Reliable, TC = Thermo-Coarse / Cheap"** — RTD is the precision instrument, Thermocouple is the rugged workhorse. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Resistance of a Pure Metal vs. Temperature (Simplified) Starting from classical Drude–Lorentz free-electron theory, the resistivity $\rho$ of a metal is inversely proportional to the mean free path $\lambda$ of conduction electrons: $$\rho \;=\; \frac{m v}{n e^{2} \lambda}$$ As temperature $T$ rises, phonon density grows roughly linearly: $$\frac{1}{\lambda(T)} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda_0}\,(1 + \alpha T)$$ Substituting: $$\rho(T) \;=\; \rho_0\,(1 + \alpha T)$$ Since $R = \rho \dfrac{L}{A}$ and geometry $L/A$ is constant: $$\boxed{\,R(T) \;=\; R_0\,(1 + \alpha T)\,}$$ This is the **linear RTD model**, valid for limited spans (typically 0 to 100 °C for first-year labs). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — PT100 Resistance Calculation (7 marks standard) **Problem (KTU-style):** > A PT100 RTD has $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ and $\alpha = 0.00385\ \text{°C}^{-1}$. Find its resistance at $T = 150\ \text{°C}$ using (a) the linear model, and (b) the Callendar-Van Dusen equation. Comment on the deviation. ### (a) Linear Model Solution $$R_T \;=\; R_0\,(1 + \alpha T)$$ Substitute $R_0 = 100$, $\alpha = 0.00385$, $T = 150$: $$R_T \;=\; 100 \times \bigl(1 + 0.00385 \times 150\bigr)$$ $$R_T \;=\; 100 \times \bigl(1 + 0.5775\bigr)$$ $$R_T \;=\; 100 \times 1.5775$$ $$\boxed{R_T \;=\; 157.75\ \Omega}$$ ### (b) Callendar-Van Dusen Solution For $0 \le T \le 850\ \text{°C}$: $$R_T \;=\; R_0\,[\,1 + A T + B T^{2}\,]$$ with $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$. $$R_T \;=\; 100 \times \bigl[\,1 + (3.9083\times 10^{-3})(150) + (-5.775\times 10^{-7})(150)^{2}\,\bigr]$$ Compute term-by-term: - $A T = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 150 = 0.586245$ - $B T^{2} = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 22500 = -0.012994$ Sum: $$1 + 0.586245 - 0.012994 \;=\; 1.573251$$ $$\boxed{R_T \;=\; 157.3251\ \Omega}$$ ### Deviation Analysis $$\Delta R \;=\; 157.75 - 157.33 \;=\; 0.42\ \Omega$$ $$\Delta T_{\text{equiv}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta R}{\alpha R_0} \;=\; \frac{0.42}{0.00385 \times 100} \;\approx\; 1.09\ \text{°C}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Students often *only* quote the linear value. For 7-mark problems, you must (i) state the linear equation, (ii) plug in numbers, (iii) show the Callendar-Van Dusen form, (iv) plug in, and (v) compute the deviation. Skipping the deviation costs you a full mark. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Cold-Junction Compensation for a Type K Thermocouple (7 marks standard) **Problem:** > A Type K thermocouple measures a hot junction at $T_h = 600\ \text{°C}$. The reference (cold) junction is at $T_c = 25\ \text{°C}$. The instrument performs CJC using the Law of Intermediate Temperatures. Using reference values $E(600,0) = 24.905\ \text{mV}$, $E(25,0) = 1.000\ \text{mV}$, find (a) the *raw* EMF the thermocouple actually produces, and (b) the temperature the instrument will *display*. ### (a) Raw EMF by Law of Intermediate Temperatures By the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures**: $$E(T_h, T_c) \;=\; E(T_h, 0) \;-\; E(T_c, 0)$$ $$E(600, 25) \;=\; E(600,0) \;-\; E(25,0)$$ $$E(600, 25) \;=\; 24.905 \;-\; 1.000$$ $$\boxed{E_{\text{raw}} \;=\; 23.905\ \text{mV}}$$ ### (b) Displayed Temperature The instrument *adds back* the cold-junction EMF (CJC) to recover the equivalent "0 °C reference" value: $$E_{\text{display}} \;=\; E_{\text{raw}} + E(T_c, 0) \;=\; 23.905 + 1.000 \;=\; 24.905\ \text{mV}$$ This corresponds (per reference table) to: $$\boxed{T_{\text{display}} \;=\; 600\ \text{°C}}$$ This confirms the *measurement* is correct, but only because the instrument "knew" the cold-junction temperature and added 1.000 mV back. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU viva:** If you forget to do CJC, your displayed temperature will be 25 °C *less* than the true value — a common lab mistake when students use handheld meters without CJC enabled. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Static Characteristics) | Test Temp (°C) | RTD Resistance (Ω) — Linear | RTD Resistance (Ω) — CVD | TC Type K EMF (mV) | Sensitivity (dE/dT or dR/dT) | |---:|---:|---:|---:|---:| | 0 | 100.00 | 100.00 | 0.000 | αR₀ = 0.385 Ω/°C ; S ≈ 40 µV/°C | | 50 | 119.25 | 119.40 | 2.022 | Linear | | 100 | 138.50 | 139.16 | 4.096 | Linear | | 150 | 157.75 | 157.33 | 6.138 | Begins curving | | 200 | 177.00 | 175.86 | 8.137 | Slight bend | | 300 | 215.50 | 212.05 | 12.207 | Clear curvature | | 600 | 331.00 | 313.65 | 24.905 | Strongly nonlinear | | 1000 | 485.00 | 485.00 (extrapolated) | 41.269 | Saturated | > [!NOTE] > The RTD row values are *calculated from the same two equations used in Worked Example 1*; the Type-K EMF values are taken from the **NIST ITS-90 reference table** for Type K thermocouples. ### Interpretation for KTU Graph - **RTD plot ($R$ vs. $T$):** A near-perfect straight line from 0 to ~500 °C. Slope ≈ 0.385 Ω/°C. - **Thermocouple plot ($E$ vs. $T$):** A gentle curve. Slope starts ≈ 40 µV/°C at 0 °C, peaks around 250 °C, then *decreases* at very high T. - **Comparison statement (must appear in your lab record):** "The RTD is more linear but limited in range; the thermocouple is non-linear and low-output, but covers a far wider temperature range and is self-powered." # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB src1["Heated Oil Bath
with stirrer"]:::heater src2["PT100 RTD
probe immersed"]:::sensor src3["Type K Thermocouple
probe immersed"]:::sensor src4["Wheatstone Bridge
with 3-wire lead comp"]:::circuit src5["RTD Transmitter
4 to 20 mA output"]:::circuit src6["Cold Junction Comp
Block with PT1000"]:::circuit src7["Thermocouple
Amplifier uV to V"]:::circuit src8["ADC and
Microcontroller"]:::processor src9["Digital Display
and PC Graph"]:::display src10["Calibrated Mercury
Thermometer 0.1 C"]:::ref src1 --> src2 src1 --> src3 src1 --> src10 src2 --> src4 src2 --> src5 src3 --> src6 src3 --> src7 src4 --> src8 src5 --> src8 src6 --> src7 src7 --> src8 src8 --> src9 src10 -. reference .-> src9 classDef heater fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B07000,color:#000 classDef sensor fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#003F7F,color:#000 classDef circuit fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef processor fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000 classDef display fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000 classDef ref fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Data Acquisition & Plotting Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR a["Step 1: Switch on bath
and stirrer"]:::s --> b["Step 2: Set temperature
to 30 C hold 2 min"]:::s b --> c["Step 3: Read both
sensors and thermometer"]:::s c --> d["Step 4: Record values
in observation table"]:::s d --> e["Step 5: Increment T
by 10 to 20 C"]:::s e --> f{"Reached 150 C
or max limit"}:::d f -- No --> b f -- Yes --> g["Step 6: Plot R vs T
and E vs T graphs"]:::p g --> h["Step 7: Compute slope
and sensitivity"]:::p h --> i["Step 8: Compare with
theoretical values"]:::p i --> j["Step 9: Discuss error
and conclude"]:::p classDef s fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000 classDef d fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000 classDef p fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid — RTD 3-Wire Bridge Compensation (Logic Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB p1["RTD element R_T"]:::r p2["Lead resistance r1"]:::r p3["Lead resistance r2"]:::r p4["Lead resistance r3"]:::r b1["Fixed Resistor R1"]:::b b2["Fixed Resistor R2"]:::b b3["Fixed Resistor R3"]:::b vx["Excitation V_ex"]:::v out["V_out to amplifier"]:::o gnd["Ground"]:::g vx --- p1 p1 --- p2 p2 --- p3 p3 --- p4 p4 --- b1 b1 --- b2 b2 --- b3 b3 --- gnd p2 -. junction .-> out p4 -. junction .-> out b2 -. junction .-> out b3 -. junction .-> out classDef r fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 classDef b fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef v fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 classDef o fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000 classDef g fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > In the 3-wire bridge, lead resistances $r_1$ and $r_3$ appear in *opposite* arms of the bridge and therefore cancel out in the differential output. This is why 3-wire (and the more accurate 4-wire Kelvin) configurations are used for long cable runs in industrial plants. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 marks each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCMUL607] **Differentiate between an active and a passive transducer. Classify RTD and thermocouple accordingly.** **Model Answer (≈ 5 lines):** An *active transducer* generates its own electrical output (no external excitation) using energy from the measurand. A *passive transducer* requires external excitation (voltage/current) to produce an output that depends on the measurand. **RTD = Passive** (resistance change needs excitation current); **Thermocouple = Active** (Seebeck EMF is self-generated). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCMUL607] **State the Law of Intermediate Metals and explain its significance in thermocouple instrumentation.** **Model Answer (≈ 5 lines):** *"Inserting a third (intermediate) metal into a thermocouple circuit does not change the net EMF, provided its two new junctions are held at the same temperature."* **Significance:** It allows engineers to introduce solder joints, terminal blocks, and extension wires into the thermocouple circuit without affecting measurement accuracy — which is essential for connecting field sensors to remote instruments. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the experimental procedure to study the static characteristics of an RTD (PT100) and a Type K thermocouple in the laboratory. List the apparatus required. (7 marks) **(b)** A PT100 RTD is connected in a 3-wire bridge. The bridge excitation is 5 V, fixed resistors are $R_1 = R_2 = 100\ \Omega$, and the third arm RTD is at 100 °C. Calculate the bridge output voltage. Assume $R_3 = 100\ \Omega$ initially at 0 °C for symmetry, $\alpha = 0.00385\ \text{°C}^{-1}$. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Procedure (7 marks):** **Apparatus:** PT100 RTD probe, Type K thermocouple probe, ice-bath reference, oil/water heating bath with stirrer, calibrated mercury thermometer (0.1 °C), Wheatstone bridge with 3-wire compensation, thermocouple amplifier with CJC, digital display / DMM, connecting leads, and a 0–250 °C range heating mantle. **Procedure Steps:** 1. **Set up the bath** — Fill a beaker with silicone oil (or water up to 100 °C) and place it on a heater with magnetic stirrer to ensure uniform temperature. 2. **Immerse the sensors** — Insert the PT100, Type K thermocouple, and a calibrated mercury thermometer to the same depth (≥ 50 mm) in the bath. 3. **Connect the cold-junction** — Place the reference end of the thermocouple in a 0 °C ice bath (or rely on electronic CJC in the instrument). 4. **Connect the bridge** — Wire the RTD in a 3-wire configuration across the Wheatstone bridge. 5. **Initial reading** — Set bath to 30 °C, wait 2 minutes for thermal equilibrium, record all three readings: (i) $R_{\text{RTD}}$ in Ω, (ii) $E_{\text{TC}}$ in mV, (iii) thermometer T in °C. 6. **Increment** — Raise the bath temperature in steps of 10 °C (or 20 °C) up to ~150 °C, repeating the recording at each step. 7. **Plot graphs** — Plot (a) $R$ vs. $T$ and (b) $E$ vs. $T$, both on the same temperature axis. 8. **Analyze** — Compute slope of RTD curve (Ω/°C) and of TC curve (µV/°C), compare with theoretical values 0.385 Ω/°C and ~41 µV/°C respectively. --- **(b) Bridge Output Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Calculate $R_T$ at 100 °C using the linear model: $$R_T \;=\; R_0\,(1 + \alpha T) \;=\; 100\,(1 + 0.00385 \times 100) \;=\; 100 \times 1.385 \;=\; 138.5\ \Omega$$ **Step 2:** Standard Wheatstone bridge output (assuming the RTD replaces $R_3$): $$V_{\text{out}} \;=\; V_{\text{ex}} \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_T}{R_3 + R_T} \right)$$ For symmetry at 0 °C ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 100\ \Omega$, $R_T = 100\ \Omega$), the first term equals 0.5, second term equals 0.5, so $V_{\text{out}} = 0$ — *balanced condition*. **Step 3:** With $R_T = 138.5\ \Omega$: $$\frac{R_T}{R_3 + R_T} \;=\; \frac{138.5}{100 + 138.5} \;=\; \frac{138.5}{238.5} \;=\; 0.5807$$ $$\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \;=\; \frac{100}{200} \;=\; 0.5000$$ $$V_{\text{out}} \;=\; 5 \times (0.5000 - 0.5807) \;=\; 5 \times (-0.0807)$$ $$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} \;=\; -0.4036\ \text{V} \;\approx\; -403.6\ \text{mV}}$$ The magnitude is what the instrumentation amplifier will read: $$|V_{\text{out}}| \;\approx\; 0.404\ \text{V}$$ This is the unamplified bridge output corresponding to 100 °C. In practice, an instrumentation amplifier with gain ~10 would give ~4.04 V suitable for an ADC. --- ### Question B (14 marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the Seebeck Effect. With reference to the Law of Intermediate Temperatures, derive the cold-junction compensation equation and show how a modern instrument uses it to display the correct hot-junction temperature. (7 marks) **(b)** A Type K thermocouple with reference at 0 °C has its hot junction at 400 °C. The reference is moved to a warm terminal block at 30 °C. What is the *new raw* EMF? Given $E(400,0) = 16.395\ \text{mV}$ and $E(30,0) = 1.203\ \text{mV}$. If the instrument's CJC uses a thermistor that reads 30 °C, what EMF value does the instrument compute internally? (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) Seebeck Effect & CJC Derivation (7 marks):** The **Seebeck Effect** states that when two dissimilar metals $A$ and $B$ are joined at a junction held at temperature $T_h$ and the free ends held at a reference temperature $T_c$, an EMF $E_{AB}$ is developed that is a function only of the two metals and the *temperature difference* $(T_h - T_c)$: $$E_{AB}(T_h, T_c) \;=\; f_A(T_h) - f_B(T_h) \;-\; \bigl[\,f_A(T_c) - f_B(T_c)\,\bigr]$$ By the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures**, for any intermediate temperature $T_m$: $$E_{AB}(T_h, T_c) \;=\; E_{AB}(T_h, T_m) \;-\; E_{AB}(T_c, T_m)$$ Choosing $T_m = 0\ \text{°C}$ (ice point): $$E_{AB}(T_h, T_c) \;=\; E_{AB}(T_h, 0) \;-\; E_{AB}(T_c, 0)$$ This is the **raw EMF** the thermocouple produces. To *recover* the true hot-junction temperature, the instrument (i) measures $T_c$ using an internal semiconductor sensor (or thermistor), (ii) looks up $E_{AB}(T_c, 0)$ from a stored table, and (iii) adds it back: $$E_{AB}(T_h, 0) \;=\; E_{AB}(T_h, T_c) + E_{AB}(T_c, 0)$$ The instrument then inverts this total EMF using the reference table to display $T_h$. --- **(b) Numerical CJC Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1 — New raw EMF:** Using the Law of Intermediate Temperatures with $T_m = 0\ \text{°C}$, $T_h = 400\ \text{°C}$, $T_c = 30\ \text{°C}$: $$E_{\text{raw}} \;=\; E(400, 0) \;-\; E(30, 0) \;=\; 16.395 \;-\; 1.203$$ $$\boxed{E_{\text{raw}} \;=\; 15.192\ \text{mV}}$$ **Step 2 — Instrument's internal computation:** The instrument reads the terminal block at 30 °C, so it must add the equivalent 30 °C EMF back: $$E_{\text{computed}} \;=\; E_{\text{raw}} + E(30, 0) \;=\; 15.192 + 1.203 \;=\; 16.395\ \text{mV}$$ This correctly recovers the 0 °C-referenced value: $$\boxed{E_{\text{computed}} \;=\; 16.395\ \text{mV} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T_{\text{display}} \;=\; 400\ \text{°C}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "raw" and "compensated" EMF** — the *raw* is what the *thermocouple alone* produces; the *compensated* value is what the *instrument* internally recovers using CJC. Marks are split between these two quantities. > 2. **Wrong sign convention** — some students write $E_{\text{raw}} = E(T_c) - E(T_h)$ which is the *opposite* sign. The reference is at the *lower* end, so $E_{\text{raw}} = E(T_h) - E(T_c)$. > 3. **Forgetting units** — the problem gives EMF in mV; tables are often in µV. Watch your decimal place. > 4. **Skipping the block diagram** in the procedure question — 2–3 marks are reserved for the labelled sketch. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **PT100** = 100 Ω at 0 °C, $\alpha = 0.00385\ \text{°C}^{-1}$, platinum IEC 60751. - [ ] **RTD equation (linear):** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$. - [ ] **Callendar-Van Dusen** is used for accuracy over the full range (0–850 °C). - [ ] **Thermocouple principle:** Seebeck Effect — EMF from temperature difference between two dissimilar metals. - [ ] **Three Laws:** Homogeneous, Intermediate Metals, Intermediate Temperatures (last one enables CJC). - [ ] **RTD = Passive transducer; Thermocouple = Active transducer.** - [ ] **RTD output is more linear; Thermocouple has wider range and faster response.** - [ ] **RTD sensitivity** ≈ 0.385 Ω/°C (PT100); **TC sensitivity** ≈ 41 µV/°C (Type K). - [ ] **3-wire bridge** cancels lead resistance; **4-wire Kelvin** is the gold standard. - [ ] **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** is mandatory for accurate thermocouple reading — implements Law of Intermediate Temperatures. - [ ] **RTD self-heating error:** keep excitation current ≤ 1 mA for PT100 to limit I²R heating to < 0.1 °C. - [ ] **Always include a calibrated reference (e.g., mercury thermometer) in the bath** — it is the *ground truth* against which both sensors are compared in the lab record. - [ ] **Graphs to plot:** (i) $R$ vs. $T$ (linear, slope ≈ 0.385), (ii) $E$ vs. $T$ (curved, slope varies). - [ ] **Conclusion to write:** "The RTD exhibits a near-linear positive temperature coefficient, while the thermocouple produces a non-linear Seebeck EMF; both follow their respective standards, with the RTD offering higher accuracy in a narrower range and the thermocouple offering wider range with self-powered operation."

topics.title: PLC - Simple ladder programming using PLC trainer kit
topics.content:
