How does it differ from a parabolic headlamp? **(7 marks)** **b)** List and briefly explain **any four headlight focusing methods** used in automotive service workshops. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Construction (3 marks):** A projector headlamp consists of: 1. **Ellipsoidal reflector** — has two focal points; the bulb is placed at the *first* focal point, and a *shutter/cut-off plate* is placed at the *second* focal point. 2. **Bulb** — HID (D2S/D2R) or LED module. 3. **Shutters / Cut-off plate** — a movable metal shield that physically blocks the upper half of the focused light, producing a sharp horizontal cut-off. 4. **Convex projection lens** — projects the image of the cut-off plate onto the road, producing a *very sharp* cut-off line with a well-defined "elbow". **Working (2 marks):** Light from the bulb is reflected by the ellipsoid, concentrated at the second focal point where the shutter shapes it, then projected by the lens onto the road. The result is a controlled, well-defined beam with a high contrast cut-off. **Differences (2 marks):** | Feature | Parabolic | Projector (Ellipsoidal) | |---|---|---| | Reflector shape | Paraboloid | Ellipsoid | | Cut-off generation | By lens flutes / filament offset | By mechanical shutter | | Cut-off sharpness | Moderate | Very sharp | | Light control | Lower | Higher (less stray light) | | Application | Older cars, commercial vehicles | Modern premium cars, LED/HID systems | --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Four Focusing Methods (each method = 1.5 marks, well-explained = 1.75):** 1. **Filament Seat Adjustment (Mechanical):** The bulb is moved axially inside the reflector by a small screw or spring clip until its luminous centre coincides with the focal point. Verified by observing the hot-spot sharpness on a screen. 2. **Lens Focus Ring Adjustment:** Some sealed-beam units and projector lenses have a focus ring that translates the lens along the optical axis to fine-tune the beam convergence. 3. **Optical/Photometric Aiming with Lux Meter:** A calibrated lux meter placed on a screen at standard distances (10 m, 25 m, 50 m) measures illuminance values at specific points. The lamp is adjusted until the photometric distribution matches ECE R112 / SAE specifications. 4. **Laser / Optical Aimer Method:** A headlight aiming machine (e.g., Hella, Beissbarth, Sun) is positioned in front of the vehicle. The machine projects a reference laser line onto the reflector, and the technician rotates the adjustment screws until the beam axis aligns with the reference. Highly accurate ($\pm 0.1^{\circ}$). 5. **Screen Pattern (Visual) Method:** *(Bonus)* A patterned screen with concentric rings and grids is used to visually assess beam width, throw, and cut-off sharpness. This is qualitative and is used in service workshops. 6. **Camera-Based ADAS Headlamp Alignment:** *(Modern, bonus)* Used with adaptive LED matrices. A camera captures the beam pattern on a wall, and software automatically adjusts the lamp via the CAN bus. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):** > 1. **Confusing "aim" with "focus":** Many students use the words interchangeably. Aiming is direction; focusing is sharpness. Examiners specifically award 1–2 marks for correct usage. > 2. **Forgetting the screen distance:** Most students remember 10 m, but if they are asked about SAE they must say **7.6 m / 25 ft**. Mixing them up costs 1 mark. > 3. **Wrong aim angle:** Students often write 1° instead of 1 % (i.e., 0.57°). The "1 % slope" is the percentage drop, not the angle itself. > 4. **Missing the elbow:** In an asymmetric beam the *elbow* is the lateral step in the cut-off. Forgetting it = 1 mark lost. > 5. **Bulb type confusion:** H4 has *two* filaments (high + low), H7 / H1 have *one*. Mixing these up = 1 mark. > 6. **No units:** Writing $E = I/d^{2}$ without specifying lux, candela, metres = full mark cut under KTU's "unit" rule. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Notes — must memorise for KTU 2024 ESE:** - ✅ **Aiming** = direction (up/down, left/right); **Focusing** = sharpness of beam and hot-spot size. - ✅ **Three optical sub-systems** of any headlamp: *Source → Reflector → Lens/Shutter*. - ✅ **Paraboloid focal length** $f = R/2$ where $R$ is the depth of the reflector. - ✅ **Filament at $f$** → collimated beam; **behind $f$** → divergent; **ahead of $f$** → crossing-divergent. - ✅ **ECE R112 screen distance = 10 m**; **SAE J1383 = 7.6 m / 25 ft**. - ✅ **ECE aim-down = 1 %** (≈ 0.57°), drop on screen = 10 cm; **SAE = 0.83°** (≈ 1.5 %). - ✅ **Tolerance** on aim = $\pm 0.2$ % of nominal. - ✅ **Asymmetric cut-off:** left side depressed (anti-glare), right side raised (sign visibility). - ✅ **Elbow** = step in cut-off line, typically at mid-screen, $0.20{-}0.30 \times$ main drop. - ✅ **H4 bulb:** two filaments — high beam at focal point, low beam offset down. - ✅ **H7/H1/HS1:** single filament bulbs (separate bulbs for high and low beam). - ✅ **Projector (ellipsoidal) headlamps:** use a *mechanical shutter* at the second focal point for cut-off. - ✅ **Maximum ECE low-beam intensity** = 75,000 cd; tested at reference axis 25 m ahead. - ✅ **Inverse-square law** $E = I / d^{2}$ governs road illuminance; units: lux = cd / m². - ✅ **Aimer types:** mechanical screen, optical/photometric, laser aimer, camera-based ADAS. - ✅ **Standards to quote:** AIS-010 (India), ECE R112 (Europe), FMVSS 108 (USA), SAE J1383. - ✅ **Vehicle prep for aiming:** half fuel, correct tyre pressure, level floor, driver or 75 kg on driver seat, no load in boot. - ✅ **Adjustment screws** are located at the rear of the headlamp unit, marked "U/D" (up-down) and "L/R" (left-right). - ✅ **Headlight levelling device** (manual or automatic/AFS) compensates for vehicle pitch due to load. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** In lab examinations, you will be physically performing the screen aiming. Always record: *(i)* the drop measured on screen in cm, *(ii)* the calculated aim percentage, *(iii)* the elbow position, and *(iv)* the type of bulb (H4 / H7 / LED). These four pieces of data fetch full marks.

topics.title: Interfacing the sensors and actuators using LabVIEW software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL607 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND MECHATRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing the sensors and actuators using LabVIEW software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**LabVIEW (Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench)** is a graphical, dataflow-driven programming environment developed by **National Instruments (NI)** that uses **G-programming** (a graphical language based on icons and wires instead of textual lines of code) to acquire, process, analyze, and present data from real-world physical systems.

In the context of PCMUL607, **Sensor and Actuator Interfacing using LabVIEW** refers to the systematic methodology of:
- Coupling physical transducers (sensors) and effectors (actuators) to a PC through a **Data Acquisition (DAQ) hardware layer**,
- Configuring the **DAQmx driver layer** (measurement services software),
- Designing a **Virtual Instrument (VI)** consisting of a *Front Panel* (HMI) and a *Block Diagram* (graphical code), and
- Executing real-time measurement, control, signal conditioning, and data-logging operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box — Must Memorize for ESE:**
> A *Virtual Instrument (VI)* = Front Panel (User Interface) + Block Diagram (Graphical Source Code) + Connector Pane (Icon/Connector for sub-VIs). All three are required to qualify as a complete VI in LabVIEW.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are speaking **English** and your friend speaks **Japanese**. You cannot communicate directly. So you hire a **translator** who listens to English and writes Japanese, and vice versa.

- **The PC (with LabVIEW)** speaks the language of *digital logic, numbers, and bytes*.
- **A physical sensor** (e.g., LM35 temperature IC) speaks the language of *millivolts, resistance changes, or frequency shifts*.
- **The DAQ card + DAQmx driver** acts as the **universal translator** — it listens to the sensor's analog whisper, converts it to digital numbers LabVIEW can plot, and (on the output side) translates LabVIEW's numeric commands into analog voltages that drive the **actuator** (motor, relay, solenoid).

So, **LabVIEW Interfacing = the complete "translator + dashboard" system** that lets you see (sensor) and command (actuator) the physical world from a computer screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mental Model for the Exam:** Sensor → Signal Conditioner → DAQ Card → DAQmx → LabVIEW VI (Processing) → DAQ Card → Driver Circuit → Actuator.

### 1.3 Core Physical & Standards Constants

| Quantity | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| ADC Resolution (typical NI USB-6008) | **12-bit** (4096 levels) | Quantization granularity |
| ADC Resolution (NI USB-6343) | **16-bit** (65536 levels) | High-precision bench use |
| LM35 Scale Factor | **10 mV / °C** | Linear, calibrated |
| Thermocouple Type K Sensitivity | **≈ 41 µV / °C** | At 25 °C reference |
| PT100 RTD Resistance @ 0 °C | **100 Ω** | Platinum, α ≈ 0.00385 /°C |
| DAQ Sampling Rate | **samples/second (S/s)** | Nyquist governs |
| Standard Industrial Voltage Ranges | **±10 V, 0–10 V, 0–5 V, 4–20 mA** | DAQ analog input modes |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 LabVIEW System Architecture — Layered Breakdown

LabVIEW interfacing follows a strict **five-layer pipeline**. Every signal from sensor to display traverses this stack:

1. **Physical Layer (Transducer/Sensor)**
   - Converts physical quantity → electrical signal (V, mA, Ω, Hz).
   - Examples: LM35 (T → V), LDR (Light → R), Strain Gauge (Force → ΔR/R).

2. **Signal Conditioning Layer**
   - Amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization, cold-junction compensation (CJC).
   - Required because sensor outputs are typically **low-amplitude** (mV, µV) and **noise-contaminated**.

3. **DAQ Hardware Layer (NI DAQ Card)**
   - Multi-function I/O card with:
     - **AI (Analog Input)** — 8/16/32 single-ended or differential channels.
     - **AO (Analog Output)** — for actuator command.
     - **DIO (Digital I/O)** — for switches, relays, encoders.
     - **Counter/Timer** — for frequency, PWM, pulse measurement.
   - Contains **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** and **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)**.

4. **Driver Layer (NI-DAQmx)**
   - High-level API for task creation, channel configuration, timing, triggering.
   - Functions: `DAQmx Create Task`, `DAQmx Start Task`, `DAQmx Read`, `DAQmx Write`, `DAQmx Stop Task`, `DAQmx Clear Task`.

5. **Application Layer (LabVIEW VI)**
   - Front Panel (controls + indicators) ↔ Block Diagram (graphical code).
   - Loop structures (`While Loop`, `For Loop`), shift registers, case structures, event structures, formula nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why G-Programming?** Dataflow execution means a node executes only when **all inputs are available**. This naturally models real-time measurement systems where the program reacts to incoming samples — ideal for closed-loop control.

### 2.2 Sensor Signal Categories — How They Enter LabVIEW

| Signal Type | Sensor Example | LabVIEW Input Mode | Conversion Needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage** (mV – V) | LM35, Thermocouple, Piezo | Analog Input (AI) — RSE / Differential | Scaling (V → engineering unit) |
| **Current** (4–20 mA) | Industrial pressure Tx | AI with external 250 Ω shunt resistor | V = I × R = 4–5 V scaled |
| **Resistance** (Ω) | RTD (PT100), Thermistor | AI via voltage divider / excitation | Linearization via Callendar-Van Dusen |
| **Frequency / Pulse** | Optical encoder, flow meter | Counter Input (CI) | f = pulses / time |
| **Digital Logic** (0/5 V) | Limit switch, Hall sensor | Digital Input line (DI) | Boolean read |
| **Serial / USB** | GPS, IMU module | VISA Read (COM port) | ASCII parsing |

### 2.3 Actuator Drive Modes — How LabVIEW Commands the World

| Actuator | Drive Signal | LabVIEW Output | Drive Circuit |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Motor (small) | 0–5 V or PWM | AO or DIO (PWM) | L293D / H-Bridge |
| Servo Motor | PWM 1–2 ms pulse @ 50 Hz | Counter Output (CO) PWM | Direct TTL signal |
| Stepper Motor | Step + Direction pulses | DO + CO | ULN2003 / A4988 driver |
| Relay / Solenoid | 24 V DC / 230 V AC | DO via opto-isolator + transistor | Driver PCB |
| Heater / Lamp | Variable power | AO → TRIAC / SSR phase control | Zero-crossing detector |
| Pneumatic / Hydraulic valve | 24 V DC coil | DO | Solenoid driver board |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mathematically verifiable and reproducible in LabVIEW using a Formula Node or Formula VI.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{out}} = n \times \Delta V_{\text{LSB}}$ | Reconstructed voltage from ADC count | DAQ post-processing |
| 2 | $\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{range}}}{2^N - 1}$ | ADC least-significant-bit size | Resolution calculation |
| 3 | $T\_{°C} = \dfrac{V_{\text{LM35}}}{0.010}$ | LM35 conversion (10 mV / °C) | Temperature scaling |
| 4 | $R_{\text{RTD}} = R_0 \left[1 + A\,T + B\,T^2 + C\,T^3\right]$ | Callendar-Van Dusen (PT100) | RTD linearization |
| 5 | $V_{\text{shunt}} = I \times R_{\text{shunt}}$ | 4–20 mA → 1–5 V with 250 Ω | Current loop conversion |
| 6 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} \geq 2 \times f_{\text{max}}$ | Minimum sampling rate | Anti-aliasing requirement |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}} - V_{\text{true}}}{V_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Percentage full-scale error | DAQ accuracy check |
| 8 | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{exc}} \cdot \dfrac{R_g}{R_g + R_1} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain gauge bridge output | Force / strain sensing |
| 9 | $V_{\text{thermo}} = S(T - T_{\text{ref}})$ | Thermocouple output (Seebeck) | Temperature sensing |
| 10 | $D_{\text{PWM}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{PWM}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Duty cycle for actuator drive | DC motor speed, heater |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Know the difference between **Resolution** (smallest detectable change, governed by 2^N) and **Accuracy** (closeness to true value, governed by calibration, noise, offset, gain error). Both are 1-mark short-answer favorites.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU testing benches** — LabVIEW acquires crankshaft position, throttle position, and drives injectors.
- **Biomedical instrumentation** — ECG, EMG, patient monitoring.
- **Smart manufacturing / Industry 4.0** — predictive maintenance via vibration + temperature DAQ.
- **Robotics** — vision-guided servo control.
- **Energy labs** — solar panel I–V characterization, battery cyclers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: ADC Resolution and Quantization Error

**Problem Statement:** An NI USB-6008 DAQ card has **12-bit** resolution and is configured for a unipolar input range of **0 V to +10 V**. Determine:
1. The size of one LSB ($\Delta V_{\text{LSB}}$).
2. The quantization error magnitude.
3. The voltage corresponding to ADC count $n = 1500$.

**Given:**
- Number of bits $N = 12$
- Voltage range $V_{\text{range}} = 10\,\text{V}$ (unipolar, $0$ to $+10$)
- ADC count $n = 1500$

**Step 1: Total number of discrete levels**
$$
L = 2^N = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

**Step 2: Size of one LSB**
$$
\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{V_{\text{range}}}{2^N - 1}
$$

Substituting values:
$$
\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{4096 - 1} = \frac{10}{4095}\,\text{V}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} \approx 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 2.442\,\text{mV/LSB}
$$

**Step 3: Quantization error (theoretical maximum)**
$$
E_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V_{\text{LSB}}}{2}
$$

$$
E_q = \pm \frac{2.442\,\text{mV}}{2} = \pm 1.221\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 4: Reconstructed voltage for $n = 1500$**
$$
V_{\text{out}} = n \times \Delta V_{\text{LSB}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 1500 \times 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 3.663\,\text{V}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
The DAQ card can distinguish a minimum of **2.442 mV** between two consecutive integer counts, with a worst-case round-off of **±1.221 mV**. For an LM35 sensor (10 mV/°C), this corresponds to **±0.122 °C** quantization uncertainty — a critical number for KTU lab viva questions.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: LM35 Temperature Sensor Interfacing Pipeline

**Problem Statement:** An LM35 is connected to the AI0 channel of the DAQ card (range 0–10 V, 12-bit). The LabVIEW VI reads an ADC count $n = 2048$. Determine the measured temperature in °C.

**Step 1: Convert ADC count to voltage (unipolar)**
$$
V_{\text{meas}} = n \times \Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = 2048 \times 2.442\,\text{mV}
$$

$$
V_{\text{meas}} = 5001.0\,\text{mV} \approx 5.001\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 2: Apply LM35 scale factor (10 mV / °C)**
$$
T\_{°C} = \frac{V_{\text{meas}}}{0.010\,\text{V/°C}}
$$

$$
T\_{°C} = \frac{5.001}{0.010} = 500.1\,°C
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** The LM35 is rated **+2 °C to +150 °C** (basic TO-92 package). A reading of 500 °C indicates the user has *likely inverted the wiring*, *used wrong ADC polarity*, or has a *gain error in the signal conditioner*. Always sanity-check against physical reality.

**Step 3: LabVIEW implementation snippet (conceptual logic, not code)**
- Block Diagram chain: `DAQmx Read (Analog 1D DBL)` → **Multiplication node** (× 2.442 mV) → **Division node** (÷ 0.010) → **Indicator** (Numeric Thermometer on Front Panel).

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: 4–20 mA Current Loop to Voltage Conversion

**Problem Statement:** A pressure transmitter outputs **4–20 mA** proportional to **0–10 bar**. It is interfaced via a precision **250 Ω** sense resistor. The DAQ card measures **$V = 6.0\,\text{V}$**. Determine the measured pressure.

**Step 1: Convert measured voltage to current**
$$
I = \frac{V}{R_{\text{shunt}}} = \frac{6.0\,\text{V}}{250\,\Omega}
$$

$$
I = 0.024\,\text{A} = 24\,\text{mA}
$$

**Step 2: Convert current to pressure (linear mapping)**
The 4–20 mA loop is mapped linearly:
- $4\,\text{mA} \rightarrow 0\,\text{bar}$
- $20\,\text{mA} \rightarrow 10\,\text{bar}$
- Span: $16\,\text{mA}$ for $10\,\text{bar}$, hence $1.6\,\text{mA/bar}$.

$$
P = \frac{I - 4\,\text{mA}}{16\,\text{mA}} \times 10\,\text{bar}
$$

$$
P = \frac{24 - 4}{16} \times 10 = \frac{20}{16} \times 10 = 12.5\,\text{bar}
$$

**Step 3: Check against DAQ range**
$V = 6.0\,\text{V}$ corresponds to $I = 24\,\text{mA}$, which exceeds the 4–20 mA loop maximum. Either the transmitter is faulty, or the sense resistor is wrong (should be 250 Ω for 1–5 V output). For **0–10 V DAQ range**, the resistor should be **$\frac{10\,\text{V}}{20\,\text{mA}} = 500\,\Omega$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — 250 Ω vs. 500 Ω:** Students often use 250 Ω because it produces the "textbook" 1–5 V. But if the DAQ is set to ±10 V, **500 Ω** is the correct choice to land the 20 mA signal exactly at 10 V — maximizing dynamic range and SNR.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor → LabVIEW Block Mapping

| Sensor | Physical Quantity | Output Range | DAQ Input Pin | Block Diagram Chain | Front Panel Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LM35 | Temperature | 0–1.5 V (2–150 °C) | AI0 (RSE) | Read → × 0.002442 → ÷ 0.010 | Numeric Thermometer + Waveform Chart |
| PT100 RTD | Temperature | ~100–138 Ω (0–100 °C) | AI0 via constant-current source | Read V → R = V/I → Callendar formula | Numeric + °C label |
| LDR | Light | 1 kΩ – 1 MΩ | AI0 via voltage divider | Read V → R = R₁(V_cc−V)/V | Bar Graph (Intensity) |
| Strain Gauge | Force / Strain | mV/V | AI0 differential + excitation | Read V → ε = 4V/(V_exc·GF) | Numeric Strain |
| Optical Encoder | Angular position | TTL pulses | Counter (CTR) | DAQmx Read Counter | Numeric + RPM |
| Thermocouple K | Temperature | µV to mV | AI0 differential + CJC | Read V → T = V/S + T_ref | Numeric with CJC indicator |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LabVIEW Interfacing Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end signal and control flow** for a typical LabVIEW sensor–actuator interfacing experiment (e.g., temperature-controlled fan / heater system). It satisfies the safe-identifier and label-format rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[PHYSICAL QUANTITY] --> nodeS2[SENSOR TRANSDUCER]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[SIGNAL CONDITIONER]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[DAQ ANALOG INPUT CHANNEL]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[ADC QUANTIZATION 12 BIT]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[DAQMX READ TASK]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[LABVIEW BLOCK DIAGRAM PROCESSING]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[FRONT PANEL INDICATOR DISPLAY]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS9[CONTROL ALGORITHM PID OR THRESHOLD]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS10[DAQMX WRITE TASK]
    nodeS10 --> nodeS11[DAQ ANALOG OUTPUT OR DIGITAL OUTPUT]
    nodeS11 --> nodeS12[DRIVER CIRCUIT H BRIDGE OR RELAY]
    nodeS12 --> nodeA1[ACTUATOR DC MOTOR OR HEATER]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[PHYSICAL EFFECT FAN SPIN OR HEAT]

    nodeU1[USER INTERACTION FRONT PANEL] -. SETPOINT .-> nodeS7
    nodeS8 -. VISUAL FEEDBACK .-> nodeU1
```

### 4.2 Layered Software Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeApp[APPLICATION LAYER LABVIEW VI]
    nodeAP[FRONT PANEL HMI]

    nodeSUB[SUB VI AND EXPRESS VI]

    nodeDrv[DRIVER LAYER NI DAQMX]
    nodeT[DAQMX CREATE TASK]
    nodeR[DAQMX READ]
    nodeW[DAQMX WRITE]

    nodeHW[HARDWARE LAYER NI DAQ CARD]
    nodeADC[ADC AND DAC]
    nodeDIO[DIGITAL IO LINES]
    nodeCTR[COUNTER TIMER]

    nodeApp --> nodeAP
    nodeApp --> nodeBD
    nodeBD --> nodeSUB
    nodeSUB --> nodeDrv
    nodeDrv --> nodeT
    nodeDrv --> nodeR
    nodeDrv --> nodeW

    nodeHW --> nodeADC
    nodeHW --> nodeDIO
    nodeHW --> nodeCTR
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Sensor–Actuator Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRef[SETPOINT REFERENCE] --> nodeErr[SUMMING JUNCTION]
    nodeErr --> nodeCtrl[CONTROLLER PID]

    nodeProc --> nodeFB[SENSOR FEEDBACK]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define a Virtual Instrument (VI). List its three primary components and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Virtual Instrument (VI)** is a software-based emulation of a physical measurement or control instrument, built in LabVIEW using **G-programming**. It replaces the knobs, switches, and displays of a bench-top instrument with a computer-based graphical interface.

The three primary components of a VI are:

1. **Front Panel (1 mark)** — The interactive **user interface** containing *Controls* (inputs: knobs, switches, numeric entry boxes) and *Indicators* (outputs: graphs, LEDs, numeric displays). This is what the user sees and interacts with.

2. **Block Diagram (1 mark)** — The **graphical source code** containing nodes (functions, sub-VIs, structures) wired together by wires. The code executes in a *dataflow* manner, meaning a node runs only when all its inputs are ready.

3. **Connector Pane / Icon (1 mark)** — The interface that allows the VI to be used as a **sub-VI** (reusable building block). It maps the VI's inputs and outputs to terminals on the icon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A common KTU viva question is *"Is the Front Panel alone a VI?"* The answer is **No** — without the Block Diagram, it is just a UI mockup, not a functional Virtual Instrument.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between Resolution and Accuracy of a Data Acquisition (DAQ) system. Why is a 16-bit DAQ preferred over a 12-bit DAQ for strain gauge measurements?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Resolution (1 mark):** Resolution is the **smallest change in input signal** that the DAQ can detect and digitize. It is determined by the number of bits of the ADC:

$$
\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{V_{\text{range}}}{2^N - 1}
$$

For a 12-bit ADC with ±10 V range, $\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} \approx 4.88\,\text{mV}$. For a 16-bit ADC with the same range, $\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} \approx 0.305\,\text{mV}$ — a **16× improvement**.

**Accuracy (1 mark):** Accuracy is the **closeness of the measured value to the true value**, expressed as a percentage of full scale:

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{V_{\text{measured}} - V_{\text{true}}}{V_{\text{FS}}} \times 100
$$

Accuracy is affected by offset error, gain error, nonlinearity, noise, and temperature drift — not just bit count.

**Why 16-bit for strain gauges (1 mark):** Strain gauges produce **microvolt-level outputs** (typically 1–10 mV at full scale under 10 V excitation). A 12-bit ADC's 4.88 mV resolution would be **larger than the signal itself**, making strain measurement impossible. A 16-bit ADC offers 0.305 mV resolution, sufficient to resolve strain levels down to ~1 microstrain.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern (as per syllabus):** Each Module Experiment carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** (theory/derivation) and **(b) 7 marks** (Block diagram / LabVIEW VI description / calculation). Internal choice is mandatory — examiner provides two alternative questions; student answers one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Model Solution**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a LabVIEW-based sensor–DAQ interfacing system. List the five layers and explain the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LM35 temperature sensor is interfaced to an NI USB-6008 DAQ card (12-bit, range 0–10 V unipolar). The LabVIEW VI records an ADC count of **$n = 1221$**.
   - (i) Calculate the measured voltage.
   - (ii) Convert this voltage to temperature in °C.
   - (iii) Determine the quantization error of the system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A LabVIEW-based sensor–DAQ interfacing system has the following **five-layer architecture** (Block diagram to be reproduced from Section 4.1 above):

1. **Sensor / Transducer Layer (1 mark):** Converts a physical phenomenon (temperature, pressure, light, force) into a corresponding electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance). Example: LM35 outputs **10 mV/°C**.

2. **Signal Conditioning Layer (1.5 marks):** Sensor outputs are typically low-amplitude and noisy. Conditioning includes:
   - **Amplification** (instrumentation amplifier for µV-level signals).
   - **Filtering** (low-pass anti-aliasing filter).
   - **Isolation** (opto-coupler for safety).
   - **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** for thermocouples.
   - **Excitation** for resistive sensors (RTDs, strain gauges).

3. **DAQ Hardware Layer (1.5 marks):** The **NI DAQ card** contains the **ADC**, **DAC**, **DIO lines**, and **counter/timers**. It physically samples the conditioned analog signal and digitizes it for the PC. Common examples: NI USB-6008, NI USB-6343, NI PCIe-6363.

4. **Driver Layer — NI-DAQmx (1.5 marks):** A high-level API providing measurement services. Key VIs/function blocks: `DAQmx Create Task`, `DAQmx Read`, `DAQmx Write`, `DAQmx Stop Task`, `DAQmx Clear Task`. DAQmx handles timing, triggering, scaling, and hardware handshake automatically.

5. **Application Layer — LabVIEW VI (1.5 marks):** The user-developed program with **Front Panel** (HMI) and **Block Diagram** (code). Includes loop structures, shift registers, charts for real-time plotting, and file I/O for data logging.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $N = 12$ bits, range $0$ to $+10\,\text{V}$, $n = 1221$.

**(i) Voltage calculation (2.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{V_{\text{range}}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}
$$

$$
V_{\text{meas}} = n \times \Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = 1221 \times 2.442 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{\text{meas}} \approx 2.982\,\text{V}
$$

**(ii) Temperature in °C (2.5 marks):**

$$
T\_{°C} = \frac{V_{\text{meas}}}{0.010\,\text{V/°C}} = \frac{2.982}{0.010}
$$

$$
T\_{°C} = 298.2\,°C
$$

**(iii) Quantization error (2 marks):**

$$
E_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V_{\text{LSB}}}{2} = \pm \frac{2.442\,\text{mV}}{2}
$$

$$
E_q = \pm 1.221\,\text{mV} \equiv \pm 0.122\,°C
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The system can resolve temperature changes as small as ~0.24 °C (2 × 0.122 °C) and has a measurement uncertainty of ±0.122 °C at every sample.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the procedure to interface a **PT100 RTD temperature sensor** with LabVIEW using an NI DAQ card. Include the relevant linearization equation and the front-panel design considerations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4–20 mA pressure transmitter** is connected through a **250 Ω** sense resistor to the analog input of a LabVIEW-DAQ system (range 0–10 V, 12-bit).
   - (i) Determine the voltage range seen at the DAQ input.
   - (ii) Calculate the ADC count when the pressure is **7.5 bar** (full-scale = 16 bar).
   - (iii) Determine the percentage full-scale error if the measured voltage is **2.20 V** at exactly 4 bar. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Procedure to interface PT100 with LabVIEW (5 marks + 2 for front panel):**

1. **Hardware Connection:** The PT100 is a **resistive temperature detector** with $R = 100\,\Omega$ at 0 °C and temperature coefficient $\alpha \approx 0.00385\,/°C$. Connect it in a **Wheatstone bridge** or use a **precision current source** (typically 1 mA) to excite it.

2. **Signal Conditioning:** A differential instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) senses the bridge imbalance, which is proportional to $\Delta R$. An excitation voltage of **2.5 V or 5 V** is supplied by the DAQ card's internal reference.

3. **DAQ Configuration in LabVIEW:**
   - Use **DAQmx Create Virtual Channel** with type = **AI Resistance** or **AI Voltage** (if bridge output is in V).
   - Select **Differential** mode to reject common-mode noise.
   - Set range based on expected bridge output (typically ±500 mV or ±1 V).

4. **Block Diagram Processing — Linearization (Callendar-Van Dusen equation):**

$$
R_{\text{RTD}}(T) = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100)\, T^3\right]
$$

where for PT100:
- $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\,°C^{-1}$
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\,°C^{-2}$
- $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\,°C^{-4}$ (only for $T < 0\,°C$)

For $T \geq 0\,°C$, the cubic term vanishes, simplifying to a quadratic.

5. **Block Diagram chain:** `DAQmx Read` → `Resistance.vi` or `Voltage.vi` → Formula Node (Callendar-Van Dusen, or **polynomial fit** from calibration table) → Temperature Indicator.

6. **Front Panel Design (2 marks):**
   - **Numeric Indicator** (large font, °C label, 2-decimal precision).
   - **Waveform Chart** for real-time trend (X = time, Y = temperature).
   - **Boolean LED** for alarm (red if T > 80 °C).
   - **Knob / Numeric Control** for alarm threshold.
   - **Stop Button** (Boolean control) wired to the While Loop's conditional terminal.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Voltage range at DAQ input (2 marks):**

With $R_{\text{shunt}} = 250\,\Omega$ and $I = 4$ to $20\,\text{mA}$:
$$
V_{\min} = 4\,\text{mA} \times 250\,\Omega = 1.0\,\text{V}
$$

$$
V_{\max} = 20\,\text{mA} \times 250\,\Omega = 5.0\,\text{V}
$$

So the DAQ sees **1.0 V to 5.0 V** for 0 to 16 bar.

**(ii) ADC count at 7.5 bar (2.5 marks):**

Pressure-to-current conversion (linear):
$$
I = 4\,\text{mA} + \frac{P}{P_{\text{FS}}} \times 16\,\text{mA} = 4 + \frac{7.5}{16} \times 16 = 4 + 7.5 = 11.5\,\text{mA}
$$

Voltage:
$$
V = 11.5\,\text{mA} \times 250\,\Omega = 2.875\,\text{V}
$$

ADC count:
$$
n = \frac{V}{\Delta V_{\text{LSB}}} = \frac{2.875}{2.442 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1177
$$

**(iii) Percentage full-scale error at 4 bar with V_meas = 2.20 V (2.5 marks):**

Expected voltage at 4 bar:
$$
I_{\text{expected}} = 4 + \frac{4}{16}\times 16 = 8\,\text{mA}
$$

$$
V_{\text{expected}} = 8\,\text{mA} \times 250\,\Omega = 2.0\,\text{V}
$$

Pressure from measured voltage:
$$
P_{\text{measured}} = \frac{(V_{\text{meas}}/250\,\Omega) - 4\,\text{mA}}{16\,\text{mA}} \times 16\,\text{bar} = \frac{(2.20/0.250) - 4}{16} \times 16
$$

$$
P_{\text{measured}} = \frac{8.8 - 4}{16} \times 16 = \frac{4.8}{16} \times 16 = 4.8\,\text{bar}
$$

Error:
$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{|P_{\text{measured}} - P_{\text{true}}|}{P_{\text{FS}}} \times 100 = \frac{|4.8 - 4.0|}{16} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Error} = \frac{0.8}{16} \times 100 = 5\,\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (–1 mark):** Many students compute percentage error as $\frac{0.8}{4.0} \times 100 = 20\%$ — this is *error relative to true value*, not relative to full scale. KTU's standard definition uses **full-scale (FS)** as the denominator.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to subtract 4 mA from the loop current when computing pressure. The 4 mA is the **live zero** (representing 0 bar), not a bias to be ignored.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** Using **2^N** instead of **2^N − 1** in the LSB formula. The correct formula is $\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = V_{\text{range}} / (2^N - 1)$ because the ADC has $2^N - 1$ *transitions* (intervals), not $2^N$ *states*.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Writing "DAQs are 12-bit" without specifying *unipolar or bipolar* range. The LSB value depends on the **range** as well as the **bit count**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 ESE Quick-Reference Card:**

- ✅ **VI = Front Panel + Block Diagram + Connector Pane.** All three are mandatory.
- ✅ LabVIEW uses **G-programming (dataflow execution)** — not sequential text.
- ✅ Standard DAQ analog input modes: **RSE (Referenced Single-Ended), NRSE, Differential.** Prefer **Differential** for low-noise sensor work.
- ✅ **NI USB-6008** is the standard student-grade DAQ: **12-bit, 8 AI, 2 AO, 12 DIO, 1 counter.**
- ✅ **LM35 scale factor = 10 mV / °C.** **PT100 = 100 Ω @ 0 °C**, $\alpha = 0.00385$.
- ✅ **4–20 mA loop** with **250 Ω** resistor → **1–5 V**. For **0–10 V DAQ range**, use **500 Ω**.
- ✅ **ADC Resolution** formula: $\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = V_{\text{range}} / (2^N - 1)$. **Note: $2^N - 1$, NOT $2^N$.**
- ✅ **Quantization error** is **±½ LSB** (theoretical maximum).
- ✅ **Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$. For a 50 Hz signal, sample at **≥ 100 Hz** (typically **1 kHz–10 kHz** in practice to allow anti-alias filter roll-off).
- ✅ **DAQmx task lifecycle:** Create → Start → Read/Write → Stop → Clear. Always **clear** tasks in the error-handling path.
- ✅ **Front Panel** elements are of two types: **Controls** (inputs, user-set) and **Indicators** (outputs, display).
- ✅ **Loop structures:** While Loop (with conditional terminal), For Loop (with N), Flat/Stacked Sequence (legacy). Modern code prefers State Machine pattern.
- ✅ **Shift Registers** are used to pass data between loop iterations.
- ✅ **Common DAQ errors:** open thermocouple detection (saturated reading), ground loops (use differential), aliasing (low-pass filter before ADC).
- ✅ **Actuator drive:** Never connect a motor or solenoid directly to the DAQ pin — always use a **driver stage** (L293D for DC motor, ULN2003 for relays, opto-coupler for isolation).
- ✅ **VIVA favorites:** "What is the role of the CJC for a thermocouple?" "What is the difference between RSE and Differential?" "Why do we use a sense resistor in a 4–20 mA loop?" "How do you stop a While Loop?"
- ✅ **Important Daqmx VIs to remember:** `DAQmx Create Task.vi`, `DAQmx Start Task.vi`, `DAQmx Read.vi`, `DAQmx Write.vi`, `DAQmx Stop Task.vi`, `DAQmx Clear Task.vi`, `DAQmx Timing.vi`, `DAQmx Trigger.vi`.
